4 TOW ARDS RESILIENT AND PROSPEROUS CITIES IN INDIA 1. THE GREATEST CLIMATE RISKS TO INDIA’S CITIES: FLOODING AND EXTREME HEAT Flood Risk is Growing Rapidly in Indian Cities Urban expansion has altered the flood risk profile for many locations, and more than two-thirds of the urban population is at risk. India has many cities along the coast and in the flood plains of major rivers. As urbanization increases impermeability, areas with previously low flood risk are experiencing flooding. For example, cities in the upper parts of watersheds, such as Bangalore, Hyderabad, and Indore. Pluvial flooding risk is growing more rapidly. It is projected that temperatures will increase across India throughout the 21st century, with heat waves becoming longer, more frequent, and more intense. In major Indian cities, nighttime temperatures exceed those of

temperatures will increase across India throughout the 21st century, with heat waves becoming longer, more frequent, and more intense. In major Indian cities, nighttime temperatures exceed those of surrounding rural areas by 3°C to 4°C year-round primarily because of concrete structures, roads, and other impermeable infrastructure in cities. These items absorb and re-emit solar energy at night through a phenomenon called the urban heat island effect. Hot days and nights cause illness, income loss, and death for vulnerable urban populations. Global projections forecast a significant intensification in heat stress, with up to 50 percent more hot days and nights by 2050. Nocturnal temperatures are likely to rarely fall below 25°C in some densely built neighborhoods of cities such as Chennai, Lucknow, and Surat. Low-income neighborhoods are most at risk, typically experiencing higher day- and nighttime temperatures than wealthier neighborhoods because of a greater prevalence of

Chennai, Lucknow, and Surat. Low-income neighborhoods are most at risk, typically experiencing higher day- and nighttime temperatures than wealthier neighborhoods because of a greater prevalence of heat-trapping materials and less shade, wind, and vegetation. Residents of these neighborhoods are least able to cope due to hotter homes, heat-exposed occupations, and less access to water and mechanical cooling. These hot conditions affect working conditions and create risks of cascading infrastructure failure. Hot conditions will make work in heat-exposed sectors dan - gerous for more of the year, with the annual number of working hours that are too hot to work safely in physically strenuous occupations (e.g., construction, street vend- ing) projected to increase. The impact of heat on critical infrastructure and networks may cause cascading failures that affect multiple sectors and become increasingly important as rising temperatures make air conditioning crucial to avoid heat-related

infrastructure and networks may cause cascading failures that affect multiple sectors and become increasingly important as rising temperatures make air conditioning crucial to avoid heat-related illness and death among vulnerable groups.

To address the increasing demand for urban infrastructure and services such as water supply and sanitation, public trans- port, access to housing, and solid waste management, the Government of India has initiated several new programs, including the Smart Cities Mission. Many municipal governments will benefit from improving their ability to conduct detailed risk assessment, risk-sensitive land use planning, and improving fiscal and fiduciary capacity to execute resilient capital investment projects. A key focus must be on expanding effective early warning and response systems in cities to save lives and protect valuable assets from rising climate and disaster impacts. resilience to prevent major flooding impacts in Indian cities. Managing urban flood risk requires an integrated approach. Strategies to address urban flooding should be holistic and based on the local risk profile, avoiding development patterns that increase flood risks. An integrated urban watershed-based flood resilience strategy is needed to regulate floodplain develop - ment and stormwater management in new urban areas. Investment is needed in grey or structural systems, such as drains and flood embankments, together with nature- based solutions, or non-structural measures, such as open spaces that can absorb flood water or sustainable urban drainage systems. Specific measures must be based on the unique risk profile of the city, not only to increase flood resilience, but also to ensure water storage, environmental restoration, and access to recreational areas. flood damages by 2050. Strengthening Urban Heat Stress Resilience Adapting to the growing risks of extreme heat will bring large economic and social benefits to Indian cities. Introduction of Heat Action Plans in Indian cities has saved lives and reduced economic losses. These need to be applied in all high-risk cities. Key heat mitigation actions include providing heat wave early warning systems, increasing urban tree canopies and shading, replacing heat-intensifying roofs with cool roofs, and shifting work hours. The benefit-cost ratios of adaptation interventions against heat are high, most notably for heat wave early warning and emergency management systems which offer potential health and economic benefits that exceed their costs by a ratio of more than 20:1. The economic case for investing in heat mitigation is even stronger considering the wider benefits that actions such as urban greening offer for mitigation of floods, increases in property values, reductions in air

for investing in heat mitigation is even stronger considering the wider benefits that actions such as urban greening offer for mitigation of floods, increases in property values, reductions in air pollution, and greater comfort and mental health of residents. » Implementing heat mitigation actions across Indian cities would increase GDP by up to 0.4 percent and save up to 130,000 lives a year by 2050. Note: See Chapter 2, Section 2.3 and Box 2.9 for details. Improving Urban Housing and Transport Adaptation Urban housing is highly exposed to climate impacts, but further actions can enhance resilience for existing and future dwellings. India’s housing sector is highly vulnerable to flooding, extreme heat, cyclones, landslides, and earthquakes, and there is an opportunity to promote and mainstream development of climate-resilient, green housing—simply because more than half of the housing stock needed in 2070 is yet to be built. Green housing approach can also support low carbon pathways

development of climate-resilient, green housing—simply because more than half of the housing stock needed in 2070 is yet to be built. Green housing approach can also support low carbon pathways by better conserving energy and water and utilizing renewable and recycled materials. Resilience in housing must be mainstreamed into policy, planning, and develop - ment, and private developers, builders, and users must all be engaged. Priorities are to develop an integrated policy and regulatory framework for resilient housing ecosystems and improve capacity to implement the framework at all levels of gov - ernment; develop capacity in data collection and monitoring of the sector to provide evidence to strengthen the analytical underpinning for climate resilience measures;

Urban transport is highly vulnerable to flood events, and regular maintenance and evidence-based investment in flood protection and system redundancy are essen- tial. More than one-quarter of all urban road networks in India are directly exposed to some level of flooding under a 1-in-100-year flood scenario. Even limited network exposure can result in drastic disruptions. With just 10 to 20 percent of roads inun - dated, some cities could lose more than 50 percent of their transportation systems. An increase in urban heat could reduce hours of rail operations, affecting urban travel. During flooding and extreme heat days, using public transit can become hazardous for travelers. Flood hazard mapping is essential, and key investments in maintenance and flood protection must be combined with development of alternative travel routes. A low-carbon pathway can be achieved by increasing the efficiency of the urban- ization process and the share of renewable energy in electricity production. Specifically, reduction can be best achieved through combinations of interventions, including encouraging urban compactness and density; modernizing municipal ser - vices, especially solid waste management; building green, energy-efficient

through combinations of interventions, including encouraging urban compactness and density; modernizing municipal ser - vices, especially solid waste management; building green, energy-efficient hous- ing; and investing in public transportation and moving away from fossil fuel–based transport. Urban Planning for Efficient Cities Well-planned compact cities have the potential to reduce energy consumption and GHG emissions, improving livability. Urban expansion, if not managed well, not only increases exposure to climate impacts but exacerbates urban challenges of access to basic services, congestion, and air pollution. Compact cities through integrated urban planning, and transit-oriented development approaches can reduce energy consump- tion and GHG emissions, enhance resilience, and reduce air pollution, improving liva- bility and quality of life. reduce air pollution, improving liva- bility and quality of life. 11 EXECUTIvE SUmmARy Most carbon emissions in urban areas come from energy, transport, and building sectors. Development of well-planned, compact cities accompanied by green areas, ame- nities, and clean public transportation will improve livability and the quality of life of urban residents. Many cities have limited green space per inhabitant and limited access to urban amenities such as parks and public transport. There is a need and an opportunity to manage urban expansion better, which would greatly improve urban livability. For example, a city that has more green space and tree canopy cover will have cleaner air and better walkability and better ability to adapt to heat waves. Clean public transportation and greater access to services and markets increase employ - ment. A city that can recover more quickly from disaster shocks and ensure business continuity will be more livable and more competitive. Investing in Green and Resilient Municipal Services Green, resilient municipal

can recover more quickly from disaster shocks and ensure business continuity will be more livable and more competitive. Investing in Green and Resilient Municipal Services Green, resilient municipal services, especially sustainable Solid Waste Management (SWM), are critical for efficient, low-carbon urban development. Modernization of solid waste management system covering the entire value chain from household segregation to recycling, and leveraging a wide range of technologies—such as clean collection vehicles and anaerobic digestion plants shows significant emissions reduc- tion in all cities assessed, attributable to a combination of better management of bio- degradable waste, reduced landfilling, and improved methane capture. Many Indian cities such as Indore are already benefiting from such investments, supporting green jobs and biogas production. Green and Resilient Urban Housing and Transport There are significant opportunities for improving energy efficiency, resilience, and emissions reduction in existing and new houses. The housing sector generates substantial emissions, which can be reduced through building improvements such as installation of rooftop solar panels and adopting green housing design parameters. Emissions are generated throughout the life cycle of production, construction, use, demolition, and rebuilding. Green housing design practices such as roof and wall insu- lation and efficient glazing can be incorporated into all new buildings, which would reduce energy consumption and emissions from the housing sector substantially by 2070. by 2070. Incremental costs for increasing energy efficiency and reducing emissions vary based on choice of intervention, with installation of rooftop solar panels being the most cost-effective. emissions—and better air quality—yet urban passenger travel demand is expected to be 42 percent greater by 2030. Transit-oriented develop- ment and compact city concepts can reduce emissions by up to 20 percent versus historical trends. SIGNIFICANT FINANCING AND CAPACITY-BUILDING SUPPORT REQUIRED TO DRIVE RESILIENT URBAN DEVELOPMENT The cost of resilient, low-carbon urban development is well beyond current munic- ipal spending and greatly exceeds current financing capacity. Under a conservative urbanization scenario of reaching 43 percent urbanization by 2050 and 52 percent by 2070, the total estimated investment needs of developing new urban infrastructure and services that incorporate climate-resilient, low-carbon approaches are projected to be $2.4 trillion by 2050 and $10.9 trillion by 2070. Under a moderate urbanization scenario of reaching 54 percent urbanization by 2050 and 66 percent by 2070, the total estimated investment needs of developing resilient and green urban infrastruc- ture and services are projected to be $2.8 trillion by 2050 and $13.4 trillion by 2070. See Chapter 4, Section 4.1 for more details. Current spending is well below the required

urban infrastruc- ture and services are projected to be $2.8 trillion by 2050 and $13.4 trillion by 2070. See Chapter 4, Section 4.1 for more details. Current spending is well below the required levels. Current capital expenditures in Indian cities—an overwhelming share of which is from public fiscal resources—are far below what is needed. Total capital investment averaged $10.6 billion per year from 2011 to 2018. India’s current spending on urban infrastructure and services (0.70 per - cent of GDP) is much lower than in other countries and must be substantially increased. Revenue generation by cities is low. Cities’ own-source revenue is low and remained flat at 1 percent of GDP from 2011 to 2018. Property tax collection is low and represents a far lower share of GDP (0.15 percent), compared with other countries (0.30 to 0.60 percent). In addition, many cities in India have not been able to spend their capital budgets fully in recent years and need support in increasing

compared with other countries (0.30 to 0.60 percent). In addition, many cities in India have not been able to spend their capital budgets fully in recent years and need support in increasing implementation capacity. Because cities do not track their entire climate-related spending, it is difficult to monitor levels and changes and to present a case for financing. Climate invest - ments are not tracked separately in municipal accounts, which impedes monitoring and reduces the chances of attracting financing. Based on a review of budgets and accounting statements for selected cities conducted for this report, just 2 to 3 per - cent of spending could be attributed to explicitly climate-related activities. Central, state, and municipal governments must work together to improve financ- ing for resilience measures. An urban finance strategy and road map can help iden - tify a list of key actions to increase public and private sector financing for resilience actions. National and state

for resilience measures. An urban finance strategy and road map can help iden - tify a list of key actions to increase public and private sector financing for resilience actions. National and state governments can help improve policies, regulations, and capacity building, including through climate-linked inter-governmental fiscal trans- fers (possibly performance based), and help strengthen municipalities’ fiduciary and financial management and own-source revenue performance. Indian cities need innovative funding and improved accounting for resilience mea- sures. Municipal governments can improve multiyear capital investment and financ - ing plans, develop bankable climate projects, and better engage the private sector. This includes strengthening municipal governments’ public finances, procurement, and planning; increasing implementation capacity; increasing private sector aware- ness; access to climate risk data and support for innovative business models. Some Indian cities such as Ahmedabad are already taking effective actions in this regard. New and international sources of financing for climate actions can also be explored such as carbon markets, climate funds including Green Climate Fund, Global Environment Facility, Climate Investment Fund, Adaptation Fund, as well as Green Bonds, commercial banks, sovereign wealth funds, multilateral/bilateral development finance institutions, blended finance, and philanthropic institutions. 5. ACHIEVING RESILIENT AND PROSPEROUS CITIES IN INDIA Now is the time for Indian cities and key stakeholders to invest more in resilient urban development. Indian cities must adapt to the rising impacts of urban flood- ing and extreme heat while investing in resilient and efficient municipal infrastruc - ture and services. Timely actions can avert billions of annual damages

to the rising impacts of urban flood- ing and extreme heat while investing in resilient and efficient municipal infrastruc - ture and services. Timely actions can avert billions of annual damages and losses in flooding and urban heat-related impacts and improve the quality of urban life, eco- nomic growth, and clean air. The benefits would be resilient, green, more-livable, and more-prosperous cities. A dedicated program is needed to support resilient urban development in India. Key priorities include investing in a 10-point action plan for cities, developing a national and state urban resilience program to address flooding and extreme heat, and devising a financing and implementation strategy. The investment program can also focus on enhancing institutional capacity and creating incentives to foster col- laboration and cooperation among key stakeholders. 23 01 INTRODUCTION AND OvERvIEW 1.1 URBANIZATION AND THE CLIMATE CHALLENGE India has a significant opportunity to shape urban- ization to drive climate-resilient, low-carbon, and inclusive growth. The country has the highest con - centration of medium and large cities in South Asia and is home to a globally significant urban system consisting of over 4,041 1 cities and towns of differ - ent sizes. The number of Indians residing in urban areas increased sixfold over the last 60 years, rising from 18 percent of the population in 1960 to 35 per - cent in 2020. 2 In 2020, cities hosted over one-third of the national population—around 480 million. This population is projected to almost double to 951 mil- lion by 2050, increasing to 1.1 billion by 2070. 3 These estimates are based on a moderate urbanization pro- jection of the urban population reaching 54 percent of the total by 2050 and 66 percent by 2070. India is experiencing increased temperatures consistent with global warming. Temperatures are projected to increase approximately 4.15°C by 2080- 99 under a high emissions pathway (under SSP5- 8.5—Shared Socioeconomic Pathway developed by Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change which projects high future GHG emissions), and 0.71°C in the most optimistic scenario (SSP1-1.9—Shared Socioeconomic Pathways which projects ambitious GHG emissions limit to meet 1.5 degree centigrade limit set out in the 2015 Paris Agreement). 8 While considerable uncertainty characterizes projections of local long-term future rainfall

These climate impacts are more pronounced in cities because the urban environment magnifies the event itself. For exam - ple, the concentration of concrete buildings cre- ates “urban heat islands” (UHI) which increase urban temperatures at night during heat waves. Increased pavement of urban settlements can exacerbate sur - face water impermeability and raise exposure to floods. Cities are highly interconnected systems that can be paralyzed by “cascading infrastructure failure” when key infrastructure assets are affected. For example, flooding can affect electricity transmission lines which may then lead to water and sewage system outages, and cause fire, disruption of traffic, and

Climate change-related extreme heat and urban flooding impacts are likely to grow exponentially if additional actions are not taken. Heat stress in Indian cities is intensifying and mortality from heat waves could increase by 120 percent by 2050. For India’s urban population, this would equate to a rise from 144,000 to 328,500 heat related deaths per year by 2050 under a high climate change scenario (considering both increased heat stress and urban population growth). Urban flooding related losses are projected to increased substantially in future. Annual estimated losses just from pluvial or storm water related flooding is likely to increase from $4 billion in 2023 to up to $14-30 billion by 2070 exposing up to 46.4 million people. Urban flooding has a substan- tial impact

pluvial or storm water related flooding is likely to increase from $4 billion in 2023 to up to $14-30 billion by 2070 exposing up to 46.4 million people. Urban flooding has a substan- tial impact on urban road network. For e.g. even with only 10 or 20 percent of the road inundated, some cit- ies can lose more than 50 percent of their transport system. Emissions from Indian cities, though relatively small on a per capita basis, are growing. Cities globally have higher emissions compared to rural areas because they house more people and have higher consumption. Most carbon emissions in urban areas of India come from the energy, transport, and building sectors. Urban emissions vary with multiple factors, including urban population growth, urban density and the structure of a city’s economy. Major cities are already planning for low-carbon growth, and there is additional potential to further improve low carbon development. 1.2 THE OUTLOOK Planning and investment in Indian cities need to

Major cities are already planning for low-carbon growth, and there is additional potential to further improve low carbon development. 1.2 THE OUTLOOK Planning and investment in Indian cities need to respond to the host of existing challenges while

Indian cities already face challenges in meeting growing demand in water supply, public transport, parks, sewerage, access to housing, and solid waste collection and treatment. Poor air quality and growing water scarcity are other crucial challenges affecting cities. Compounding these challenges, new urban growth in many cities is taking place largely on the city’s fringes and spilling over municipal boundaries, making it challenging to coordinate and deliver resil- ient services and infrastructure.9 The last population census, conducted in 2011, showed that 65.5 mil- lion Indians lived in informal urban neighborhoods. These informal settlements increase cities’ vulnera- bility and exposure to climate and disaster impacts, especially affecting their low-income residents. These challenges need to be addressed urgently. The Government of India (GoI) has started several initia- tives to meet urban

and disaster impacts, especially affecting their low-income residents. These challenges need to be addressed urgently. Additional investments and technical support is now needed to further improve climate-informed urban planning, regulation and enforcement, resil- ient infrastructure, and service provision as climate impacts are projected to increase dramatically in cit- ies (see earlier paragraphs). 28 TOW ARDS RESILIENT AND PROSPEROUS CITIES IN INDIA Climate change and urban settlement growth pat - terns are increasing disaster risk in Indian cities. The key factors contributing to urban disaster risk— hazard, vulnerability and exposure—are compounded by climate change and how the urbanization is taking place in India. » Some of the main hazards affecting Indian cities are flooding, landslides, earthquakes, cyclones, storm surges, glacier melt, heat waves, and water scarcity. Climate change is affecting rainfall patterns and intensity as well as temperatures, increasing flooding, extreme heat, and water scar- city impacts. » Increasing vulnerability or propensity to damage in many cities is largely driven by old infrastructure designed for a smaller population and by infra- structure design standards that take inadequate account of levels of stormwater runoff. Urban vul- nerability is also driven by new infrastructure and buildings that do not include measures to reduce extreme

design standards that take inadequate account of levels of stormwater runoff. Urban vul- nerability is also driven by new infrastructure and buildings that do not include measures to reduce extreme heat or flooding impacts. » Exposure to hazards in Indian cities is greatly increased by new settlement growth in haz- ard-prone areas. Added factors are the lower adap- tive capacity of residents living in low-lying and high-risk urban areas, for e.g. day laborers working in extreme heat who may not have access to health care and safety nets. This chapter focuses on urban flood and heat impacts as these are the hazards most intensified by climate change in India. The chapter has six sections. 11 The selected cities are: Agartala, Agra, Bengaluru, Chennai, New Delhi, Dhanbad, Guwahati, Hyderabad, Imphal, Indore, Jaipur, Kanpur, Kolkata, Lucknow, Ludhiana, Mumbai, Patna, Puri, Raipur, Sangli, Surat, Thiruvananthapuram, and Varanasi. These cities cover a range of scales in terms of population

Imphal, Indore, Jaipur, Kanpur, Kolkata, Lucknow, Ludhiana, Mumbai, Patna, Puri, Raipur, Sangli, Surat, Thiruvananthapuram, and Varanasi. These cities cover a range of scales in terms of population and area. Their populations range from under one million to around 20 million. New Delhi, Mumbai, Chennai, and Kolkata constitute the four major metro areas in the country. Six of the selected cities have populations over 10 million, three have between 4 and 10 million, six have 2 to 4 million, five have 1 to 2 million, and four have 0.1 to 1 million. Moreover, these cities not only are the centers of urbanization and engines of economic growth, but also hold political significance. Many of them are state capitals, wielding considerable power in urban planning and governance. Therefore, it is crucial to examine the disaster and climate risks they confront and understand their local exposure to natural hazards, as these can have a direct impact on the growth trajectory of the entire country.

to examine the disaster and climate risks they confront and understand their local exposure to natural hazards, as these can have a direct impact on the growth trajectory of the entire country. 12 Chennai, Surat, Lucknow, Thiruvananthapuram, New Delhi, and Indore. Sections 2.1 and 2.2 focus on identifying impacts from flooding and urban heat challenges. Section 2.3 focuses on what cities can do to improve resilience to these climate risks. Sections 2.4 and 2.5 focus on adaptation needs in housing and urban transport— sectors that are crucial to ensure inclusive and resil- ient urban development. Section 2.6 summarizes the path toward building cities adapted to climate and disaster risks. The findings in this chapter are based on rapid risk assessments undertaken for selected cities11 and on “deep dive” (or case study) analysis for six cities.12 These cities were selected based on their location, hazard profile, and size. 2.1 URBAN FLOODING IMPACTS Flooding is a growing challenge for

on “deep dive” (or case study) analysis for six cities.12 These cities were selected based on their location, hazard profile, and size. 2.1 URBAN FLOODING IMPACTS Flooding is a growing challenge for most Indian cities. Climate change is affecting the intensity, frequency and variability of rainfall events. In addi- tion, rapid urban settlement growth has created large impervious surfaces which increase runoff and reduce infiltration capacity, resulting in higher peak discharge. Existing drainage infrastructure is often only able to cope with limited rainfall intensity (e.g., <10 mm/hr) and is frequently blocked or encroached. In new development areas, there is often a lag in installing stormwater drainage infrastructure. These conditions, together with the intense rainfall associ- ated with the Indian monsoon, create frequent flood- ing in Indian cities and often result in damage and service disruptions.

29 02 CLImATE ImPACTS AND URbAN ADAPTATION NEEDS BOX 2.1 Types of Floods Urban floods can be classified into three main categories: » Pluvial flooding occurs when surface water builds up beyond the absorptive capacity of soil due to extended precipitation and insufficient drainage. Urbanization increases impervious areas and leads to construction of conduits and channels, increasing the peak flood flow. » Coastal flooding is due to tidal or storm surges or sea level rise. » Fluvial flooding occurs when intense or excessive precipitation or snow melt causes rivers to overflow. It is a natural process when, in wet years, a river leaves its lower bed and spreads water across the flood plain. Flood impacts happen when the population occupies the flood plains and is thereby exposed to flooding. In addition, flash flooding refers to quick flooding which often begins within 3-6 hours of the event. Flash flooding may be triggered by heavy rainfall, convective thunderstorms, cloudbursts,

In addition, flash flooding refers to quick flooding which often begins within 3-6 hours of the event. Flash flooding may be triggered by heavy rainfall, convective thunderstorms, cloudbursts, erosion, or dam or levee breaks. This flooding type is the most difficult to manage because it gives limited time to warn and evacuate people. Pluvial flood risk in cities is increasing more rapidly than fluvial and coastal flooding risk. This increase is driven by new settlement growth in areas exposed to pluvial floods. Settlement development patterns also contribute to growing flood risk and increasing imperviousness, blocking natural stormwater flow or drying up wetlands. Based on a recent assessment of global flood and set- tlement data (Rentschler, et al., 2022), 13 it was found that very high pluvial flood risk zones face a bigger increase in settlement extent than safer areas. While settlement extent in safe areas grew by 82 percent from 1985 to 2015, settlement extent in very high plu-

pluvial flood risk zones face a bigger increase in settlement extent than safer areas. While settlement extent in safe areas grew by 82 percent from 1985 to 2015, settlement extent in very high plu- vial flood risk areas grew by 102 percent. An assess- ment undertaken for this report for selected Indian cities14 found that risks in cities from pluvial flooding have been growing faster than from fluvial or coastal flooding. From 1985 to 2015, built-up areas grew overall by 94 percent. 31 02 CLImATE ImPACTS AND URbAN ADAPTATION NEEDS BOX 2.2 A Note on the Methodology Used to Assess Present and Future Pluvial Flood Risk for Urbanized Areas in India Pluvial flood risk analysis of direct flood damages was conducted for pluvial flood events expressed in U.S. dollars per year. Risk analysis was overlaid with residential and non-residential land use data to derive the exposure per land use type (e.g., the amount of land exposed to a given flood severity). To estimate risk, the exposure information was overlaid with flood fragility curves and maximum damage functions for land use types. The flood fragility curves and maximum damage values were derived from previous work and are widely adopted in flood risk analysis (Huizinga, De Moel and Szewczyk, 2017). Maximum damage values of $380 per m2 (2020 U.S. dollar values) were adopted for urban residential land use, and $580 per m2 for non-residential urban land use. The benefits of implementing adap - tation measures to cope

of $380 per m2 (2020 U.S. dollar values) were adopted for urban residential land use, and $580 per m2 for non-residential urban land use. The benefits of implementing adap - tation measures to cope with future expected risk were based on several simplifying assumptions. The sensitivity of the results was tested against a range of hypothetical adaptation measures (expressed in terms of the intended future protection level) and the net present value of these interventions. In this analysis, discounted net benefit was estimated over the lifetime of the adaptation option. Key data sources used for analysis: » Flood risk: FATHOM flood maps were used for analysis. These maps are derived from a global flood model for 10 different return periods (5, 10, 20, 50, 75, 100, 200, 250, 500, 1000 years) showing extent of the flood and flood depth per return period. » Land use: The JRC human settlement layer (JRC-HSL) was used for urban residential, non-residential land use and urban population. This

showing extent of the flood and flood depth per return period. » Land use: The JRC human settlement layer (JRC-HSL) was used for urban residential, non-residential land use and urban population. This is a satellite-derived population and built-up area product at 100-meter resolution. The JRC- HSL has exposure (population and built-up area) data for a 10-year time interval. Four time frames were used to eval- uate the historical change in urban flood risk [2000, 2010, 2020, 2030 (predicted)]. JRC-HSL was also used to identify urban areas based on the urban classes adopted in the model: urban, dense, semi-dense, and suburban. This analysis should be interpreted with caution on a local scale. First, pluvial flooding is influenced by the local context, such as the presence of storm drains and local variations in soil conditions. and local variations in soil conditions. 32 TOW ARDS RESILIENT AND PROSPEROUS CITIES IN INDIA Climate change and rapid urban settlement growth are the main drivers of future pluvial flood risk, with a forecasted increase of 3.6–7 times in the area exposed by 2070. Based on an assessment under - taken for this report (see Box 2.2), climate change is likely to exacerbate pluvial flooding in many areas, potentially increasing pluvial flood risk in Indian cit - ies by 73– 100 percent over the next half-century. Climate models project an intensification of extreme precipitation over most of India, except for coastal areas in the southwest and southeast that may expe- rience a limited or even negative change in extreme precipitation. A secondary but also important driver of increased flood risk comes from patterns of urban growth. Most Shared Socioeconomic Pathways (SSPs)19 project a strong urbanization in high-risk areas, driving a steep increase in pluvial flood risk. Adaptation measures to pluvial flood risk should therefore

Shared Socioeconomic Pathways (SSPs)19 project a strong urbanization in high-risk areas, driving a steep increase in pluvial flood risk. Adaptation measures to pluvial flood risk should therefore primarily focus on areas combining both climate change threats and urbanization patterns. Concentrating on areas where large climate impacts and future urbanization are expected, ‘hotspots’ where adaptation measures are most needed have been identified for this report. Coastal Floods Storm surge and tropical cyclone events can cause an exceptional increase in sea level and trigger coastal flooding in coastal cities. India is frequently affected by tropical cyclones (typhoons), particularly during the monsoon season that runs from June to October. On average, around two to four tropical

in coastal cities. India is frequently affected by tropical cyclones (typhoons), particularly during the monsoon season that runs from June to October. On average, around two to four tropical cyclones impact India every year, mostly affecting West Bengal, Odisha, Andhra Pradesh, and Tamil Nadu. The Bay of Bengal to the east of India is one of the most cyclone-prone areas in the world, with an average of five to six tropi - cal cyclones forming in the region each year. Of India’s 7,500 km coastline, 5,700 km are exposed to severe cyclones and flooding, and 40 percent of India’s total population lives within 100 km of the coastline, mostly in dense urban settlements.20 The highest risk of mortality and damage in built-up areas from coastal flooding events is in West Bengal due largely to very high population densities in and around Kolkata, where most of the built-up area is exposed to both coastal and pluvial flooding. 21 TABLE 2.1 Urban Pluvial Flood Risk in India: Estimated Impacts

Fluvial Floods The fluvial flood hazard is a serious threat to many Indian cities. Weather patterns affected by climate change, combined with the abundance and size of major river channels, mean that many cities are highly exposed to fluvial flooding. It is estimated that, if noth- ing changes, annual damages due to fluvial flooding in India’s cities will increase from $21 billion in 2010 to $220 billion in 2030 and $770 billion by 2050 (WRI Aqueduct Floods, 2023). The most exposed cities and states are those crossed by the major river systems notably the Ganga-Brahmaputra. These include cities and towns in Bihar and northern parts of the coun - try including Uttar Pradesh, Assam, and West Bengal. Other flood-prone areas are in Rajasthan, Delhi, Kerala, and Punjab. Urban expansion and the construction

Bihar and northern parts of the coun - try including Uttar Pradesh, Assam, and West Bengal. Other flood-prone areas are in Rajasthan, Delhi, Kerala, and Punjab. Urban expansion and the construction of water management infrastructure have significantly altered the fluvial flood risk profiles of Indian cit - ies. The density of river networks in India, notably the Ganges and its tributaries, means that most cities are located near a river and are potentially exposed to fluvial flooding. The analysis of flood risk in selected Indian cities carried out for this report (see Figure 2.4) concluded that the extent of built-up areas exposed to fluvial flood hazards has almost doubled between 1985 and 2015 to reach 1,683 square kilo - meters by 2015.24 This pattern is particularly notable Mumbai Kolkata Chennai Surat Agartala Guwahati Puri Thiruvananthapuram 0 50 100 150 200 21002050

In the case of riverine cities, the expansion on both sides of rivers made possible by modern bridge technolo - gies has significantly increased exposure to flooding. Attempts to reduce exposure in large cities like New Delhi and Surat have included the construction of embankments which have contained water but may have resulted in increased flood heights for down - stream cities. These absorb solar energy and re-emit it at night, leading to higher temperatures, a phenom- enon called the UHI effect. Infants and people over 75 in low-income households are particularly susceptible to heat waves. High vul- nerability and high exposure to extreme heat tend to co-occur in Indian cities. Individuals with char - acteristics that make them more likely to fall ill or die during heatwaves tend to live in locations where average temperatures are higher, most notably infor- mal settlements. Hotter conditions will make work in heat-exposed sectors dangerous for more of the year, reduc - ing economic output. 40 TOW ARDS RESILIENT AND PROSPEROUS CITIES IN INDIA Growth in demand for cooling will place strain on the power sector but also create economic oppor- tunities. With growth in both urban populations and temperatures, the demand for cooling to keep people within safe temperature zones—including in homes, workplaces, and during transportation journeys—will increase. Aggregate nationwide cooling demand measured in tonnage of refrigeration is projected to rise eightfold by the late 2030s against a present-day baseline. This will be driven by an 11-fold increase in space-cooling demand as well as smaller rises in cooling demand for agricultural cold-chain, refriger - ation, and transport air-conditioning.36 With high temperatures posing an increasingly critical threat to human health, the power sector will face ris- ing challenges in meeting cooling energy demand. The sector will have to reduce outages, which will increas- ingly pose dangers to human life during peak tempera- tures. It

sector will face ris- ing challenges in meeting cooling energy demand. The sector will have to reduce outages, which will increas- ingly pose dangers to human life during peak tempera- tures. It will also have to meet the cooling demand of poorer and marginalized citizens. However, the growth in demand for cooling services across the built envi - ronment, agricultural supply chains, and transporta - tion also presents significant economic opportunities that can promote job creation and economic growth. 36 Cell, O. "India cooling action plan." New Delhi: Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change, Government of India, 2019. Available at: http://www. ozonecell. com/viewsection.jsp. Finally, the impact of heat on critical infrastruc - ture and networks may cause cascading failures that affect multiple sectors. For instance, extreme heat may lead to an electricity distribution network failure, causing a series of secondary effects like stranded trains, compromised safety measures

affect multiple sectors. For instance, extreme heat may lead to an electricity distribution network failure, causing a series of secondary effects like stranded trains, compromised safety measures at railway crossings, delays in getting to work, hospitals with a lack of network power, and failing information and communication technology (ICT) infrastructure. Because of the thermal properties of steel, which expands and buckles when air temperatures exceed 36°C, Indian urban transport systems have already faced significant delays during heatwaves due to the buckling of railway tracks. The number of days per year during which trains cannot safely operate is projected to increase by 30 percent by 2050 in Surat. There will clearly be a need to adapt urban transpor - tation equipment, systems design, and operations for future heat extremes (see Figure 2.10). Cascading infrastructure failures are challenging to predict due to complex interdependencies, but their associated costs, such as

design, and operations for future heat extremes (see Figure 2.10). 41 02 CLImATE ImPACTS AND URbAN ADAPTATION NEEDS 2.3 PLANNING AND MANAGEMENT TOOLS FOR URBAN RESILIENCE AND ADAPTATION This section focuses on key planning and manage- ment tools that municipalities can use to improve urban adaptation and resilience capacity. The sec - tion addresses: (i) municipal capacity, systems, and planning; (ii) climate and disaster risk assessment; (iii) warning and response systems; (iv) integrated urban flood risk management; and (v) urban heat adaptation and preparedness. Municipal Capacity, Systems, and Pro-poor Planning Local government capacity and systems improve- ments are at the heart of achieving effective urban adaptation. Many Indian cities are already incorpo - rating measures to improve urban resilience, but the needs are huge and more actions are required. One key area of focus for urban adaptation is to support the most vulnerable. Urban poverty reduction and adaptation to climate change are closely interlinked. Simulations were conducted to

required. One key area of focus for urban adaptation is to support the most vulnerable. Urban poverty reduction and adaptation to climate change are closely interlinked. Simulations were conducted to evaluate the impacts of climate change on people’s welfare at a household level. While aggregate impacts are highly uncertain, poorer people will be disproportionately impacted by climate change, with the bottom 20 percent of the population experiencing climate change losses that are 70 percent larger than the top 20 percent.37 This is particularly true of impacts from disasters, with poor people and households exhibiting much lower socio- economic resilience and thus much higher losses in terms of well-being while rich households bear most of the losses in absolute monetary terms. For example, the bottom 20 percent is found to experience 60 per- cent higher well-being losses due to a 100-year flood compared with the average population. This vulnera- bility has many causes including

the bottom 20 percent is found to experience 60 per- cent higher well-being losses due to a 100-year flood compared with the average population. This vulnera- bility has many causes including poorer people’s loca- tion making them more exposed and socioeconomic characteristics such as occupation, share of expen- ditures on food, and access to health care, financial instruments, or social protection. 37 Staff calculations based on the ShockWaves microsimulation methodology using household consumption microdata from the Consumption Pyramid Household Survey, World Bank CCKP, Sartori, et al., 2016 (for labor productivity), Colon-Gonzalez, et al., 2022 (for health), and IFPRI IMPACT model 2017 (for agriculture). Climate and Disaster Risk Assessment for Impact- based and Inclusive Warning, Preparedness, and Risk Reduction Climate adaptation measures need to be based on dynamic risk profiles of specific urban areas. Many Indian cities are already undertaking risk assess- ment. These need to

and Risk Reduction Climate adaptation measures need to be based on dynamic risk profiles of specific urban areas. Many Indian cities are already undertaking risk assess- ment. These need to be mainstreamed into land use planning approaches and updated regularly. This will help improve risk sensitive urban planning and avoid development patterns that increase exposure to flooding or other disaster risks. Risk classifica- tions should be a function of relative historical urban flood risk and relative increases in risk. National and state strategies for urban resilience, as well as city- level adaptation plans, need to be developed based on these risk profiles. At the state and city levels, risk information can help with adaptation plans to withstand and recover from the impacts of specific hazards. This includes, for instance, flood zoning and contingency plans to deal with heatwaves. Risk information will help identify robust and inte- grated adaptation measures covering both green and

This includes, for instance, flood zoning and contingency plans to deal with heatwaves. Risk information will help identify robust and inte- grated adaptation measures covering both green and grey infrastructure rather than standalone approaches. For example, assessment of the spe- cific risks of urban flooding can help identify a combi- nation of measures including nature-based solutions (NbS) and “sponge city” approaches, as well as the provision, retrofitting, and improved maintenance of key infrastructure services such as urban drainage, SWM, and stormwater management systems. Urban heat impacts can similarly be addressed through a combination of green and grey approaches such as increasing urban greening, shifting work hours for workers, introducing cool roofs, and the development of early warning systems. The latter two interventions have particularly high cost-benefit ratios. Cities need to prioritize vulnerable neighborhoods based on risk information when implementing these

of early warning systems. The latter two interventions have particularly high cost-benefit ratios. Cities need to prioritize vulnerable neighborhoods based on risk information when implementing these measures to improve the resilience of areas and populations most susceptible to climate stressors.

42 TOW ARDS RESILIENT AND PROSPEROUS CITIES IN INDIA Cities can also improve adaptation through bet - ter local planning based on risk information. Better land use planning and regulations based on regularly updated risk maps can avoid unplanned growth and steer urban expansion to safer areas. Urban resil- ience planning should embed risk reduction into urban expansion projects and promote NbS, natural hydrolog- ical systems, and urban tree canopy coverage. Further, cities should adjust not only where and what they build but also, crucially, the way in which they build. Building standards and codes need to be improved and better enforced. Cities should, for example, promote the use of natural ventilation, impervious surfaces, and con - struction of flood-resistant homes using local mate- rials. Provisions should be incorporated into relevant legal and regulatory frameworks. Impact-based and Inclusive Warning and Response Systems Urgent attention is needed to strengthen urban early

rials. Provisions should be incorporated into relevant legal and regulatory frameworks. Impact-based and Inclusive Warning and Response Systems Urgent attention is needed to strengthen urban early warning and response systems to save lives and valuable assets. The one top priority where Indian cities can save lives and assets from rising climate and disaster impacts is to invest more in the entire value chain of early warning and response systems. This would include improving impact-based and inclusive multi-hazard warning and emergency response systems; improving last-mile connectivity and community preparedness; developing emer - gency response and recovery systems; and ensuring safety nets for poor and low-income households. In India, there is already an effective early warning system in place for tsunamis and cyclones. Among many success stories, 1.55 million coastal residents in Odisha were evacuated during Cyclone Fani in 2019 thanks to an early warning system developed under

in place for tsunamis and cyclones. Among many success stories, 1.55 million coastal residents in Odisha were evacuated during Cyclone Fani in 2019 thanks to an early warning system developed under the National Cyclone Risk Mitigation Project I. Many Indian cities have also developed flood or heat action plans and have made progress toward functioning early warning and response systems. Cities can build on the improvements in national flood forecasting and warn- ing to develop inclusive and impact-based warning and response systems. The following are the key areas to focus on in the development of these systems: » Local multi-hazard risk assessment: Systematically invest in dynamic and multi-hazard local risk assess- ment (for key hazards such as flooding, landslides, fire, etc.) that can be combined with hydrometeorological data to develop impact-based, multi-hazard warning. » Emergency response capacity: Train and equip professionals such as ambulance and fire brigades for emergency

be combined with hydrometeorological data to develop impact-based, multi-hazard warning. » Emergency response capacity: Train and equip professionals such as ambulance and fire brigades for emergency response. » Community preparedness: (i) Understand community behavior and barriers, especially the most marginalized and vulnera- ble communities. (ii) Involve communities in identifying safe areas and shelters. (iii) Develop networks of volunteers and first responders. (iv) Undertake regular drills in high-risk areas. In 2013, a group of researchers experimented with community-based flood early warning systems in 25 vulnerable communities along the Singora and Jiadhal rivers. The experiment showed that these systems not only gave residents more lead time to protect their lives and assets, but also empowered communities to take ownership of the systems. The same could be done in urban areas to allow for the strengthening of critical infrastructure and for timely evacuation in case of

but also empowered communities to take ownership of the systems. The same could be done in urban areas to allow for the strengthening of critical infrastructure and for timely evacuation in case of need: » Post-disaster response and recovery: Have resources and mechanisms in place to support quick post-disaster response. There is also an opportunity to equip critical community resources (e.g., schools and hospitals) with stronger disaster protection and turn them into resilience hubs in times of need. » Access to safety nets: Have the provision of social protection system including cash transfers, in-kind assistance, and public works program to protect and support the most vulnerable and poor popula- tion especially in time of crisis. » Institutional strengthening: Have a dedicated local government cell with skilled staff and immedi- ate access to emergency funds. Generally, district and local governments will play the key coordinat - ing role in the warning period and after.

43 02 CLImATE ImPACTS AND URbAN ADAPTATION NEEDS Integrated Urban Flood Risk Management Now is the time to plan and prevent major flood impacts in Indian cities. With India’s urban population projected to double to 951 million by 2050 and reach 1.1 billion by 2070, a total of 45,000 km2 of new urban areas may be added between 2023 and 2050, and as much as 75,000 sq. km between 2023 and 2070. If not managed well, this massive increase in the urban area will substantially increase flood impacts while also leading to contamination of water resources and water supply and sanitation networks, leading to consequent health hazards and reduced quality of life. Examples from countries such as Brazil, which have gone through a similar urban transition (see Box 2.3), show that it is critical to carefully manage the transition and invest early on in integrated flood risk management systems. Strategies to cope with urban flooding should be based on the local risk profile of urban areas, avoiding

manage the transition and invest early on in integrated flood risk management systems. Strategies to cope with urban flooding should be based on the local risk profile of urban areas, avoiding development patterns that increase flood risks. Integrated urban flood risk manage- ment at national and state levels with sustainable stormwater management at city level is recom - mended. Integrated management would include: (a) regulation to control the flood flow increase due to expansion of new urban areas; (b) zoning of flu- vial flood areas based on flood risk maps and urban development zoning; (c) preparation of stormwater plans for the city using structural solutions in major drainage; and (d) creation or improvement of a local stormwater institution to implement all the mea- sures and to develop operation and maintenance of the stormwater infrastructure, with a revenue stream to cover the costs. Investment needs will depend upon the specific risk profile of the urban areas and on the

develop operation and maintenance of the stormwater infrastructure, with a revenue stream to cover the costs. Investment needs will depend upon the specific risk profile of the urban areas and on the interventions chosen. A conservative estimate of the costs of cov- ering 60 percent of high-risk cities nationwide over BOX 2.3 Moving Toward Integrated Urban Flood Risk Management Solutions in Brazil Over half a century, Brazil went through a major urban transition with the urban population increasing from 37 per - cent of the total in 1950 to 81 percent in 2000. During this time, cities grew in area but with scant investment in urban infrastructure and with unplanned informal settlements springing up on city fringes. This led to higher flood- ing impacts, contamination of water sources and supply, and water scarcity. Earlier approaches to managing river basins and floods that had worked at lower population levels had to be drastically changed, moving from mostly structural measures such

and water scarcity. Earlier approaches to managing river basins and floods that had worked at lower population levels had to be drastically changed, moving from mostly structural measures such as channels to more integrated solutions that combined both structural and nonstructural measures such as detention ponds, flood zoning, and storm water regulations. For example, in São Paulo, until the 1980s the main flood management solution was to construct channels. This was not only a costly method, but it also increased the flooding risk. It changed after 1980s when 42 people drowned in floods in Belo Horizonte, and the municipality adopted a new flood resilience solution based on developing retention ponds. However, the use of these ponds in major drainage systems requires better integration with sanitation ser - vices, because the ponds can deteriorate with inflows of sewage and garbage. Now many Brazilian cities have moved toward integrated flood risk management with sustainable

with sanitation ser - vices, because the ponds can deteriorate with inflows of sewage and garbage. Now many Brazilian cities have moved toward integrated flood risk management with sustainable stormwater management. Another example is from the city of Porto Alegre in Brazil which relies on three lines of actions: (a) regulations to control flood flow increase due to urbanization from new urban areas. All new urban constructions are regulated by storm water regulation to reduce or avoid flood damages of approximately $3 million/year; (b) stormwater plan developed for each urban watershed basin (26) and regulated through provision of detention ponds and other structural and non-structural measures; and (c) a storm water utility company was established to implement actions (a) and (b). 45 02 CLImATE ImPACTS AND URbAN ADAPTATION NEEDS national, state, and local urban flood risk mitigation policies, action plans, and investment strategies. Research undertaken for this report included a risk classification system to identify priority areas for urban flood risk reduction, as well as to pre- pare intervention strategies. The risk classification is based on two indicators: (i) relative urban pluvial flood risk in 2020 and (ii) the change in urban plu- vial flood risk between 2000 and 2020. Four basic strategies were identified based on the level of risk matched with the rate of change: » Where the current flood risk is high, but rate of change in risk is low (i.e. cities are not expanding in high-risk areas), invest in strengthening flood preparedness and retrofitting of flood protection systems » Where current flood risk is low, but are likely to face higher flood risk in future (for example due to rapid settlement expansion in areas prone to flooding), improve flood

protection systems » Where current flood risk is low, but are likely to face higher flood risk in future (for example due to rapid settlement expansion in areas prone to flooding), improve flood preparedness and nature- based solutions such as green areas or parks that can absorb excess flood water » Where current flood risk is low and is likely to stay low in future, strengthen urban infrastructure and improve maintenance including for urban drain - age system (so blocked drains don’t lead to urban flooding) » Where the current flood risk is high, and future risk will be even higher, urgently invest in integrated urban flood risk management including flood risk sensitive planning (so new settlements are not in flood prone areas), strengthen flood preparedness The Ahmedabad Municipal Corporation, Gujarat formed a Special Purpose Vehicle, the Sabarmati Riverfront Development Corporation Limited, which has supported river front development and urban flood resilience activities. ©

47 02 CLImATE ImPACTS AND URbAN ADAPTATION NEEDS The total adaptation benefits for a relatively achievable 10-year plan to protect against pluvial flooding range from $10 billion to $45 billion (see Figure 2.12)—but a holistic approach is needed to deal with residual risks. Even under a conservative cost-benefit ratio of one, large investment needs are required to cope with the increasing pluvial flood risk over the next 30–50 years. More importantly, resid- ual risks remain, which are a magnitude of several times greater than the present-day risks. Investing in flood protection alone is not sufficient and needs to be supplemented with additional measures to reduce residual risk, such as flood-proofing the housing stock, emergency management, and insurance or other financial instruments. A holistic approach is required for India to cope with the growing pluvial flood risk. Urban Heat Adaptation and Preparedness The introduction of Heat Action Plans (HAPs) in Indian cities has saved

A holistic approach is required for India to cope with the growing pluvial flood risk. Urban Heat Adaptation and Preparedness The introduction of Heat Action Plans (HAPs) in Indian cities has saved lives and reduced loss of eco- nomic output, but implementation of heat mitigation actions remains limited across most urban areas. Ahmedabad’s pioneering HAP—which links early warn - ing systems with actions to protect vulnerable groups during heatwaves— is credited with averting around 1,200 deaths per year in the city during the five years following its introduction in 2013 (Hess, et al., 2018). With GoI support, HAPs are being rolled out country - wide, with more than 100 local HAPs now in place. Stronger implementation of heat mitigation actions across Indian cities would yield major benefits. For urban greening, the benefit–cost ratio of 1.2:1 (see Figure 2.14) indicates that every dollar spent on tree planting yields a positive return on investment as the reduction in extreme heat exposure and brings health and

benefit–cost ratio of 1.2:1 (see Figure 2.14) indicates that every dollar spent on tree planting yields a positive return on investment as the reduction in extreme heat exposure and brings health and productivity impacts. As of 2020, it was estimated that nearly half of India’s urban population lived in informal settlements,42 with many of these located in flood-prone areas. According to the 2011 Census, one-quarter of dwellings in informal settlements had metal or asbestos roofs, making them more sus- ceptible to hazards like extreme heat. 43 Inhabitants of these informal settlements are overwhelmingly low-income households with few alternative housing options and are more vulnerable to climate disaster risks than other groups (see Box 2.5 and Box 2.6). 51 02 CLImATE ImPACTS AND URbAN ADAPTATION NEEDS BOX 2.6 Informal Settlements with High Vulnerability to Flood Risks in Chennai, Tamil Nadu Chennai, the capital of the state of Tamil Nadu, is India’s fourth largest city located on the southeast coast by the Bay of Bengal. Floods have historically affected Chennai due to a combination of reasons, including: (a) heavy rainfall during the monsoon months; (b) post-monsoon cyclones and storm surges near the Bay of Bengal; and (c) tsunamis from the Indian Ocean that have inundated coastal communities. In the past two decades, the city has suffered two severe floods: (1) the 2004 Indian Ocean Mega Tsunami with waves up to 30 meters high, which affected 73,000 people and damaged 17,000 houses in Chennai; and (2) the 2015 floods, which caused 400 deaths and an economic loss of over $2 billion. The city has been expanding rapidly in the past decades and unplanned growth and informal set - tlements have taken place in areas unsuitable for housing

and an economic loss of over $2 billion. The city has been expanding rapidly in the past decades and unplanned growth and informal set - tlements have taken place in areas unsuitable for housing development. These informal settlements (including slums both officially notified and not yet notified by government) typically have higher density than average, are built with lower-quality materials and roofing, and lack sufficient access to basic infrastructure and services, such as storm - water drainage, sewerage, or sanitation. As a result, inhabitants of these informal settlements are at the highest risk and most vulnerable to urban floods and extreme heat. Since the 2015 floods, the city started marking flood-prone areas and identifying settlements that need to be relocated. Under the “Housing for All” program of the GoI, the Government of Tamil Nadu (GoTN) developed a long-term strategic plan, allocating Rs. 65,000 crores (USD $ 7.7 billion) to provide housing and infrastructure

“Housing for All” program of the GoI, the Government of Tamil Nadu (GoTN) developed a long-term strategic plan, allocating Rs. 65,000 crores (USD $ 7.7 billion) to provide housing and infrastructure development for households currently living in slums in the city (TNUHDB, 2023). A recent study shows that more than two-fifths of the slum population would be inundated at the time of floods of a return period of five years, and three-fifths of all Chennai’s slums would be inundated by floods of a return period of 25 years. In the extreme case, when the city is hit by a flood of a return period of 100 years, it is estimated that nearly one million people, more than two-thirds of all those living in slums, would be affected (Chennai City Action Plan, 2023). agencies at all levels of government. India’s National Housing and Habitat Policy, introduced in 2007 now includes a range of environmental factors including climate change. At the building level, there are several policies and building codes issued by national agencies. 46 Although the measures are largely focused on energy efficiency, some adaptation actions include, setting minimum building envelope performance standards to limit heat gains and heat loss. Several states have updated and adopted Building and Development Control Rules to address climate risks and enhance sustainability (see Table 2.3).47 There have also been initiatives at the community level. Furthermore, robust mech - anisms are required to improve compliance with BOX 2.7 Mainstreaming Affordable Cool Roofs for the Urban Poor in India Among the techniques to promote climate resilience in the housing sector, cool roofs are one of the most cost- effective and easily accessible options. “Cool roofs” can be a passive design technique, such as the application of cool roof paints and/or certain reflective roof materials. These techniques can reduce solar heat gain absorbed by buildings, cooling the inside while limiting the energy consumption and CO2 emissions that cooling by air condition- ers and fans entails. » At the building level, effective cool roofs can reflect as much as 80 percent of the sunlight on a clear day, and a typical cool roof can bring down the indoor air temperature by around

entails. » At the building level, effective cool roofs can reflect as much as 80 percent of the sunlight on a clear day, and a typical cool roof can bring down the indoor air temperature by around 3-5°C. Cool roofs also increase the durabil- ity of the roof itself by reducing thermal expansion and contraction. » At the city level, mainstreaming cool roofs on all types of buildings can diminish the UHI effect substantially, con- tributing to climate adaptation and mitigation. Cool roofs also help in mitigating climate change by reflecting back into space solar radiation that would otherwise be converted into infrared radiation and become trapped in the atmosphere, contributing to global warming. Given its population density, rapid growth in buildings, and tropical climate zone, India has a high potential for adopting cool roofs. Because cool roofs are cost-effective and easy to fit, they have considerable potential both for existing housing stock and for new housing, as well as for

a high potential for adopting cool roofs. Because cool roofs are cost-effective and easy to fit, they have considerable potential both for existing housing stock and for new housing, as well as for retrofitting to housing in informal settlements and so benefitting the urban poor. The India Cooling Action Plan 2019 (ICAP) promotes cool roofs, recommending them for low-income housing schemes such as the Pradhan Mantri Awas Yojana (Urban) - Housing for All program. The ICAP proposes that the ener- gy-efficient building envelope guidelines of the Eco-Niwas Samhita (Energy Conservation Building Code- Residential) be enforced and that specific cool-roof programs receive government financial support. In response, the Natural Resources Defense Council (NRDC) of India is collaborating with many city and state governments to implement and mainstream cool roofs. kilome- ters of road network in 28 Indian states and highlights the most vulnerable cities, as well as those remaining comparatively unaffected by hazardous floods. The data and results of this study support a better under- standing of flood risks to urban road networks and mobility patterns in India, allowing identification and prioritization of protection and resilience measures. Figure 2.16 shows that even with only 10 or 20 percent of the road network inundated, some cities can lose more than half their transport system because 50 per- cent of trips become impossible to complete. These results show that these indirect mobility impacts, such as failed trips, can far exceed the direct impacts of network exposure. The vulnerabilities of urban transport networks vary, depending on their redundancy and structure, with some cities showing very high vulnerability. Several cities have route failure rates of over 50 per- cent even for very frequent floods (return period of 5 years),

redundancy and structure, with some cities showing very high vulnerability. Several cities have route failure rates of over 50 per- cent even for very frequent floods (return period of 5 years), including Tulsipur (Uttar Pradesh), Patna (Bihar), Iltifatganj (Uttar Pradesh), Tirumakudalu Narasipura (Karnataka), and Cumbum (Tamil Nadu). For major floods (e.g., 1-in-100 year return period), almost all cities show high shares of route failure. Among the cities with the highest share of failed routes in this extreme scenario are Tulsipur (Uttar Pradesh), Iltifatgani (Uttar Pradesh), Jatani (Odisha), and Garhmuktesar. (Uttar Pradesh). Priorities for Action Improve the maintenance of infrastructure assets, including drainage systems and culverts. To reduce flooding and persistent damage from flood- ing, maintenance of infrastructure systems is the most cost-effective solution. This is particularly the case for roads, with high benefits from improving the maintenance of drainage systems and

ing, maintenance of infrastructure systems is the most cost-effective solution. This is particularly the case for roads, with high benefits from improving the maintenance of drainage systems and culverts. Analyzing Organisation for Economic Cooperation and Development (OECD) member countries, the

56 TOW ARDS RESILIENT AND PROSPEROUS CITIES IN INDIA World Bank’s Lifeline report on resilient infrastructure shows that every additional $1 spent on road mainte- nance saves on average $1.50 in new investments. 49 Maintenance not only ensures that assets can with - stand extreme events, but also that new investments in road infrastructure can be reduced.50 Increase system redundancy. The resilience of infra- structure needs to be evaluated at the system level, not at the asset level. For instance, with increased road redundancy, roads that become impassible during floods can be replaced by alternative routes. If there is more than one route to reach a certain location, flooding of a road has only limited impacts on the functionality of the total road network. This is particularly important to ensure that critical infra- structure like hospitals and schools remain acces- sible during a crisis or an extreme weather event. However, increasing road density needs to be eval- uated and

to ensure that critical infra- structure like hospitals and schools remain acces- sible during a crisis or an extreme weather event. When exposure can - not be avoided, the design of infrastructure assets should be driven by two considerations: (1) the criti- cality of the asset (proxied by the economic and wel- fare impacts if the asset fails); and (2) the exposure of the asset (i.e., the likelihood of the asset being affected by various hazards). The availability and use of the most recent climate risk data is key to aligning policies and investments with current risks and to protecting roads and infrastructure from flood risks (Schuetz, et al., 2023). 2.6 BUILDING CITIES ADAPTED TO CLIMATE AND DISASTER RISKS Indian cities have a brief window of opportunity to leverage urbanization to improve resilience to cli- mate change impacts. With the rapid development and expansion of cities and infrastructure expected in the coming half-century, there is an opportunity to maximize development gains by ensuring that all urbanization decisions and

rapid development and expansion of cities and infrastructure expected in the coming half-century, there is an opportunity to maximize development gains by ensuring that all urbanization decisions and investments take current and future risks into account. This chapter has iden- tified key actions that could deliver high economic returns and contribute to reduced climate change impacts. For instance, investing in urban flood miti- gation can avert the massive costs of flood damages and losses annually. Greening cities with cool roofs and implementing heatwave early warning systems would be expected to save more than 50,000 lives per year by 2050 and have a high benefit-cost ratio (up to 50:1 for the early warning systems). Clearly, India’s cities must integrate adaptation and resilience within their core urbanization policies and plans. Six key recommendations and conclusions emerge from the analysis (see Box 2.9). The measures discussed in Box 2.9 will need to be better incorporated

their core urbanization policies and plans. Six key recommendations and conclusions emerge from the analysis (see Box 2.9). The measures discussed in Box 2.9 will need to be better incorporated into city planning, budgeting, and management, and this will require considerable institutional strengthening. The planning capacity BOX 2.8 Priorities for Improving Urban Mobility Resilience in Chennai, Tamil Nadu Chennai’s transport and mobility system is affected by floods and cyclones. Other climate shocks are also growing in importance and magnitude, including heatwaves and sea level rise (with commensurate increases in storm surges). At the higher strategic level, there are three important messages for consideration by the city of Chennai regarding floods and urban transport: First, urban transport resilience requires strong inter-agency coordination at the municipal and upstream river basin level. Many of the structural and non-structural measures needed to enhance urban transport and

resilience requires strong inter-agency coordination at the municipal and upstream river basin level. Many of the structural and non-structural measures needed to enhance urban transport and mobility resilience are the responsibility of city departments other than those directly responsible for transport. In the case of upstream or basin-wide issues, other administrations need to be involved. Second, the impact of floods on the transport and mobility system cannot be analyzed in isolation from the condi- tions and challenges of that system in dry conditions. Floods amplify the existing bottlenecks and inefficiencies of the city’s transport and mobility system. Segments and nodes of the urban road network that face frequent traffic congestion or are close to capacity will frequently be the areas most impacted under flood conditions. A practical first step to reducing the impact of floods is to study, understand, and tackle the bottlenecks and inefficiencies of the system in dry

be the areas most impacted under flood conditions. A practical first step to reducing the impact of floods is to study, understand, and tackle the bottlenecks and inefficiencies of the system in dry conditions. Third, continuous data collection is critical to identifying priority challenges and to studying structural and non-structural solutions. Chennai is fortunate to have a wealth of information on its transit and mobility system. However, building the resilience of the transit and mobility system will require the continuous gathering of data that are usually not collected by city transport departments in India. This data includes: (i) weather data and predictions; (ii) urban hydrological data; (iii) vulnerability data on critical transport infrastructure links to floods; and (iv) mobility data during flood conditions.

This can best be achieved through a six point agenda focusing on: (i) better assessing and tracking evolving risks through urban risk profiles; (ii) improving disaster preparedness including early warning, response and recov- ery measures; (iii) ensuring risk-sensitive and compactness considerations in new urban area development; (iv)

(ii) improving disaster preparedness including early warning, response and recov- ery measures; (iii) ensuring risk-sensitive and compactness considerations in new urban area development; (iv) developing, implementing, and maintaining integrated risk reduction measures; (v) supporting the poorest and most vulnerable citizens, including through targeting and safety nets; and (vi) facilitating the private sector’s role in risk transfer and resilience of private assets. The following paragraphs discuss each point. (i) Urban risk profiles. Cities need to pursue dynamic and comprehensive climate and disaster risk assessments to better understand evolving risks spatially. These assessments have been conducted infrequently. Of the 126 cities assessed by the Climate-Smart Cities Assessment Framework,52 only 10 had conducted flood risk assessments and developed flood management plans, six coastal cities are undertaking ward-level flood risk analyses and developing resilience action plans.53

only 10 had conducted flood risk assessments and developed flood management plans, six coastal cities are undertaking ward-level flood risk analyses and developing resilience action plans.53 These assessments are crucial to provide the information necessary for planning impact- based early warning, identifying new land for development, undertaking risk reduction and mitigation measures, and improving private sector involvement in insurance provision. The assessments can also help avoid locking in development patterns that increase vulnerability and exposure to climate impacts. (ii) Early warning, response, and recovery capacity. These are quick-win and cost-effective investments that save lives and protect property. Locally informed risk information or risk profiles should be incorporated into early warn- ing and emergency response. Many Indian cities are already investing in improving early warning and response, but major implementation gaps remain. Improving impact-based warning

into early warn- ing and emergency response. Many Indian cities are already investing in improving early warning and response, but major implementation gaps remain. Improving impact-based warning using localized risk data will be crucial to provide actionable early warning for at-risk communities. Many cities are already acting in this regard. For example, following the success of Ahmedabad’s pioneering HAP,54 further HAPs are being rolled out. The approach needs to be replicated nationwide and the quality of the HAPs and their implementation needs strengthening. It is crucial to
