With the urban population in India set to double by 2070, it is critical that all growing cities focus on supply of new urban land based on local climate and disaster risk profiles, and that they take measures to ensure risk-sensitive development with access to resilient services, infrastructure, and buildings. In the case of flood risk reduction, this requires good zoning for flood areas and other hazards. New developments must include pro- vision to reduce exposure to flood hazard and to improve stormwater management. National and state governments have made good progress in providing state and district level “risk atlases”(for e.g. flood hazard maps or atlases for states provided by National Disaster Management Authority of India. have made good progress in providing state and district level “risk atlases”(for e.g. flood hazard maps or atlases for states provided by National Disaster Management Authority of India. See https://ndma.gov.in/flood-hazard-atlases). There is an opportunity to develop such more detailed maps for all at-risk cities. Land use plans for new areas need to be based on risk identification that includes surrounding areas. Some urban development authorities are already systematically adopting risk-sensitive analyses in their master plans and are providing building approval based on risk assessment. Similar approach needs to be adopted for all cities that are at a high risk of flooding, water scarcity and/ or extreme heat impacts. (iv) Integrated urban risk reduction measures. The complexity of the climate adaptation challenge requires a focus on integrated rather than standalone approaches, spanning both grey and green infrastructure. This requires development, implementation, and maintenance

climate adaptation challenge requires a focus on integrated rather than standalone approaches, spanning both grey and green infrastructure. This requires development, implementation, and maintenance of comprehensive climate and disaster risk mitigation measures or adaptation plans and integrating them with the urban development paradigm. This chapter’s six sections investigate key measures that cities can take to follow a low-carbon development pathway. Section 3.1 provides an overview of the emissions trajectory in Indian cities. Section 3.2 uses growth modeling of selected cities to focus on the benefits from compact city planning, managed densifica- tion, and improvements in urban amenities. Section 3.3 focuses on what is needed to achieve green and resilient municipal services, especially in sustain - able waste management. Section 3.4 investigates opportunities in the housing and transport sectors. Section 3.5 focuses on the shifts needed in the urban transport sector to ease air pollution and the related potential health benefits. Section 3.6 summarizes the 56 "Thriving: Making

These sectors accounted for 64 percent of the total emissions in the cities analyzed. India’s cities have tended to spread horizontally and to become less dense on average, and this has tended to increase emissions more than a compact development pathway would have. Urban emissions vary with multiple factors, including urban density and the structure of a city’s economy. In recent years, India’s urban built-up areas have grown in size and have become less compact: the average density of the selected cities assessed decreased by 4 percent 57 The selected cities are: Agartala, Agra,

India’s urban built-up areas have grown in size and have become less compact: the average density of the selected cities assessed decreased by 4 percent 57 The selected cities are: Agartala, Agra, Bengaluru, Chennai, New Delhi, Dhanbad, Guwahati, Hyderabad, Imphal, Indore, Jaipur, Kanpur, Kolkata, Lucknow, Ludhiana, Mumbai, Patna, Puri, Raipur, Sangli, Surat, Thiruvananthapuram, and Varanasi on average between 2010 and 2020. For several rea- sons, this pattern of less-compact growth has tended to increase emissions more than a denser pattern of development would have. Horizontal growth entails converting agricultural land into peri-urban areas, which leads to higher carbon emissions and the need for more infrastructure. Lower-density expansion also results in higher energy use and increased travel with consequent higher emissions. BOX 3.1 Analysis Using Urban Growth Scenarios for Five “Deep Dive” Cities The urban growth scenarios aim to portray different urban planning paths by 2030 and 2050 for the five “deep dive” cities of Chennai, Indore, New Delhi, Surat, and Thiruvananthapuram. The scenarios were built with statistical models, spatial data, and the identification of key interventions for urban expansion, emission reduction, and adaptability. Urban Growth Scenarios: The urban growth scenarios under this analysis are defined by establishing a baseline and

of key interventions for urban expansion, emission reduction, and adaptability. Urban Growth Scenarios: The urban growth scenarios under this analysis are defined by establishing a baseline and three alternative scenarios to obtain projections for 2030 and 2050, as detailed below: » The Historical Trends scenario explores how the cities would perform with no significant changes in current investment patterns or urban policy. There are limited investments in new schools, health centers and public spaces, and no new public transit lines. Financial resources are concentrated on building roads and other infra- structure in the expansion areas. » The Plan scenario explores the potential performance of the cities following national and local development plans. Assumptions under this scenario include key climate change mitigation and adaptation measures described in national and local development plans and moderate efforts at regulation to support compact growth. » The Best Case scenario

include key climate change mitigation and adaptation measures described in national and local development plans and moderate efforts at regulation to support compact growth. » The Best Case scenario explores the potential performance of cities following ambitious urban policies to reach net-zero goals. Investments under this scenario aim for gradual decarbonization transition to a low carbon path- way by implementing: (a) compact growth; (b) energy efficiency; (c) clean transport infrastructure; and (d) green infrastructure (that prioritize environmentally sustainable and low carbon approaches). Policy Levers: Policy levers trigger changes in the performance of a city. In this analysis, four major policy levers are considered: (1) compact and resilient city planning; (2) improved urban amenities and sustainable mobility; (3) energy efficiency strategies; and (4) efficient water and waste management. Each of these levers can achieve several objectives through sets of policies and

amenities and sustainable mobility; (3) energy efficiency strategies; and (4) efficient water and waste management. Each of these levers can achieve several objectives through sets of policies and investments.

Under the Best Case scenario, a significant greenfield area of potentially 345 km2 could be preserved from urbanization by 2050: 105 km2 in Chennai, 29 km2 in Indore, 136 km2 in New Delhi, 67 km2 in Surat, and 8 km2 in Thiruvananthapuram. This would contribute to decreasing carbon emissions as it is proven that compact cities significantly reduce energy consumption (Ahlfeldt, 2018). Moreover, under the Best Case scenario in the five cities, the population’s exposure to hazards could significantly decrease by 2050. To achieve this, urban densities should be improved through compact and

under the Best Case scenario in the five cities, the population’s exposure to hazards could significantly decrease by 2050. To achieve this, urban densities should be improved through compact and resilient city planning strategies that promote higher buildings, carry out urban regeneration projects, factor risks into planning, and prioritize housing development in suitable locations. Improve urban amenities related to non-car mobility, green infrastructure, and education and health. Improving non-car mobility through more public transport and public spaces provides a significant opportunity to reduce emissions. If the improvement of public transport and public spaces is integrated into a perspective of green infrastructure and NbS, these projects could also reduce vulnerability to hazards within cities. Promote electrification of transport, improve energy efficiency in public lighting and buildings, and increase renewable energy generation Energy-efficient technologies reduce the demand for electricity, heating, and power generation, potentially reducing fossil fuel consumption and emissions. reduce the demand for electricity, heating, and power generation, potentially reducing fossil fuel consumption and emissions. For example, based on the analysis of five cities, energy consumption can be reduced by up to 56 percent compared to the Base Case scenario. In addition, shifting from fossil-fueled energy generation to renewable energy supports the reduction of carbon emissions. Improve water and waste management by replicating and scaling up local best practices Water is critical for tackling the vulnerability of cities because of the expected rising temperatures that increase demand for water. The problem is most acute for the urban population living in informal settlements with limited access to water. Under the Best Case scenario, 6,823 million liters of water per day could be saved by 2050 in the five cities analyzed. Waste management is also critical for mitigation in cities as it accounts for 32 percent of methane emissions. Innovative initiatives have emerged in New

be saved by 2050 in the five cities analyzed. Waste management is also critical for mitigation in cities as it accounts for 32 percent of methane emissions. Innovative initiatives have emerged in New Delhi, Chennai, Bengaluru, Surat, and Thiruvananthapuram, which could be replicated and scaled up. Climate resilient, compact, and better-performing city development is cost- effective in the long term Figures 3.4–3.6 show that the Best Case scenario requires extra investment for adaptation and mitigation compared to the Historical Trends scenario. However, these investments would allow for savings in expansion costs. For example, in Chennai, the scenario modeling suggests savings on urban expansion costs that outweigh the costs of mitigation and adaptation-related investments in the Best Case scenario.

Between 2010 and 2020, most of the selected cities analyzed experienced population growth in the range of 4–30 percent. The increase was due predominantly to natural increase of the population and inward migration, together with administrative changes that included new populations through declassification of rural areas and boundary changes. 70 TOW ARDS RESILIENT AND PROSPEROUS CITIES IN INDIA the development patterns of India’s cities compared to other countries, with a predominance of low- to middle-rise buildings. For the future, expansion is an option but the evidence is that urban expansion, especially if it is uncontrolled and sprawling, will con- sume forests, agricultural lands and water resources, cause stress on ecosystems and lead to air and higher impacts from climate induced disasters. On the other hand, uncontrolled densification exacerbates urban challenges of access to basic services, congestion, and air pollution. However, if accompanied by proper urban planning, green areas, amenities and public transport, compact cities have the potential to reduce energy consumption and greenhouse gas emissions and to improve livability. Under the Historical Trends scenario, urban densities (inhabitants per km2) in the five cities are likely to increase 2020-2050 by 6–29 per- cent (except in Thiruvananthapuram). Under

livability. Under the Historical Trends scenario, urban densities (inhabitants per km2) in the five cities are likely to increase 2020-2050 by 6–29 per- cent (except in Thiruvananthapuram). Under the Best Case scenario, urban density in the five cities would increase faster over the same period, by as much as 20 percent in New Delhi and by over 60 percent in Indore and Surat [see Figure 3.5 (a)]. There is an opportunity to improve efficiencies in Indian cities through a combination of “com - pact cities,” mixed-use development, and transit- oriented development (TOD), all of which serve both mitigation and adaptation purposes. The Indian urban densification process has been charac- terized by low to middle-rise buildings, many of them two to three stories [see Figure 3.5 (b)]. The densifi- cation process results more from informal urbaniza- tion patterns than from vertical urban development. In the selected cities analyzed, the current densifi- cation process follows several different

cation process results more from informal urbaniza- tion patterns than from vertical urban development. In the selected cities analyzed, the current densifi- cation process follows several different patterns: (i) infill processes where vacant land or green areas are developed with new buildings within the existing urban area; (ii) suburban areas being urbanized, with increasing densities; and (iii) new developments with increasing building heights and densities and sup - porting mixed land uses. While efficiency strategies will differ by urban characteristics, many cities are already exploring the various planning tools high - lighted in Table 3.2 to ensure the development and management of efficient cities. Better managing urban expansion and investing in green and resilient buildings are critical for achieving efficient cities. © ah_fotobox.

This is critical for controlling urban expansion and ensuring that appropriate land is set aside to ensure access to urban mobility and basic services as cities grow in the long term. Land Use Zoning Restrict and regulate the type of activities that will be acceptable on particular land lots such as residential, commercial, industrial, open/green, and public space, among others. This will allow cities to: (i) manage negative externalities from industries (such as pollutants, GHG and methane emissions); (ii) control exposure to vulnerabilities (such as

space, among others. This will allow cities to: (i) manage negative externalities from industries (such as pollutants, GHG and methane emissions); (ii) control exposure to vulnerabilities (such as construction in flood-prone zones), create buffers to urban flood, and ensure green canopy coverage for resilience to urban heat; and (iii) enforce necessary environmental clearances. Floor Area Ratio (FAR) or Floor Space Index (FSI) Zoning Utilize FAR/FSI zoning (which is used to regulate and control building heights, size, and occupancy) to plan overall requirements for floor space to ensure efficient use of land and to provide incentives to developers to finance public amenities (such as a park, green/open space, or right-of-way). Development Control Regulations (DCR) Enforce DCRs that ensure that development conforms to spatial development plans, policy guidelines, regulations, and standards issued by the planning authorities. Measures may include requirements for affordable housing,

that development conforms to spatial development plans, policy guidelines, regulations, and standards issued by the planning authorities. Measures may include requirements for affordable housing, parking, amenities, and other features to ensure that development is orderly and serves the welfare of the public. Building Codes and Guidelines Enforce building codes for the design and construction of buildings to ensure long-term resilience and mitigation (including energy and water efficiencies). Land Value Capture (LVC) LVC enables communities to recover and reinvest land value increases that result from public investment and government actions. While various LVC instruments (such as land readjustment, land value taxes, transfer of development rights, etc.) have had varying levels of success, LVC, if applied well, can be a self-financing and sustainable model for TOD and urban infrastructure development. Source: World Bank staff. 3.3 GREEN AND RESILIENT MUNICIPAL SERVICES Successfully pursuing low-carbon development pathways will require investing in the provision of green and resilient municipal services. This sec - tion focuses on SWM as an example of such a ser - vice because it provides opportunities to reduce GHG emissions, strengthen resilience, and improve public health outcomes. Globally, municipal SWM is respon- sible for 5 percent of carbon dioxide emissions and 20 percent of human-induced global methane. 59 Inadequate SWM leads to blocked urban drainage sys- tems, aggravating flooding events and contributing 59 "What a Waste 2.0." Improving waste management systems can sig- nificantly support transition to low carbon path - way. Indian cities produce 55 million tons of solid waste, and are projected to produce 285 million tons by 2035 and 415 million tons by 2050 with great potential to modernize SWM system and reduce GHG emissions (based on More Growth, Less Garbage, World Bank, 2021). of municipal SWM services. Waste dumping needs to be stopped and modern treatment and disposal capacities need to be introduced. The use of a results-based approach could incentivize greater achievement of emission reduction targets.65 Reducing emissions from the SWM sector will require a roadmap with clear priorities, milestones, and timeframes. Given the basic state of current urban SWM in India and the rapidly increasing vol - umes of waste, significant investments will be needed simply to sustain the current standard of waste man- agement. Establishing a well-performing system with environmental controls in place that can absorb increasing volumes over time will be essential to mod- erate GHG emissions. Approaches should be based on the waste hierarchy with efforts to minimize waste generation through both behavior change and recy - cling and reuse. Investments should focus on separa- tion and disposal infrastructure along with closure of dumpsites. Table

to minimize waste generation through both behavior change and recy - cling and reuse. Investments should focus on separa- tion and disposal infrastructure along with closure of dumpsites. Table 3.4 summarizes the roadmap. The regulatory, policy, and operational underpin - nings also need to be developed for the introduction 65 These may focus on: (a) modernizing the disposal capacity; (b) efficiency of waste minimization and diversion activities; (c) capacity and quality of disposal systems; and (d) financial sustainability through cost recovery. of other treatment solutions. Although several laws, policies, standards, and guidelines on SWM exist, India needs a fully integrated and coherent frame- work and enforcement of regulations. Improvements are needed in: (i) technical and emission standards for disposal facilities; (ii) policies for waste mini- mization and diversion from landfills; and (iii) fiscal tools that could improve cost recovery and financial predictability. standards for disposal facilities; (ii) policies for waste mini- mization and diversion from landfills; and (iii) fiscal tools that could improve cost recovery and financial predictability. India should develop regulations to stop open dumping and introduce mandatory design specifications and standards for the development, operation, and monitoring of disposal infrastructure. Urban local bodies (ULBs) need to develop priority investment plans to upgrade disposal infrastructure and need to strengthen technical capacities for the operation of disposal infrastructure. Investment in SWM that reduces emissions can bring other benefits too. 77 03 OPPORTUNITIES fOR EffICIENT, RESILIENT AND LOW-CARbON URbAN DEvELOPmENT 3.4 GREEN AND RESILIENT HOUSING The housing sector generates substantial emis- sions through the life cycle of production, con - struction, household use, and demolition and rebuilding. This section analyzes the mitigation potential of the housing sector in terms of house- hold energy use. Using the EDGE software devel- oped by the International Finance Corporation (IFC), the World Bank team collected data from five “deep dive” cities (Chennai, Indore, New Delhi, Surat, and Thiruvananthapuram) as input values. Models were structured to estimate different outcomes and impacts on energy consumption and GHG emis- sions under three different scenarios. 66 The model- ing results and their interpretation are summarized below. » Scenario 1, the Historical Trends scenario, assumes the continuation of historical trends in building design and energy consumption of households. » Scenario 2, the Retrofitted or Betterment

These changes include four key improvements (see Box 3.2): (i) urban compactness; (ii) improving municipal services, especially SWM; (iii) green and energy efficient housing; and (iv) invest - ing in public transport, as well as increasing resource efficiency. The Best Case scenario assessed in this report estimates that carbon emissions could decrease by as much as 80 percent between 2020 and 2050. In the four cities reviewed (Chennai, Indore, New Delhi, and Surat), this would require multiple changes by 2050: average urban density to increase by 41 percent by 2050; the amount of waste treated to reach 75 percent; access to amenities to increase by well over 400 percent; average energy consumption to decrease by 45 percent; and average water con - sumption to go down by 8 percent. To realize the full mitigation potential of India’s cities, regulatory, policy, and operational mea- sures need to be developed. For

percent; and average water con - sumption to go down by 8 percent. To realize the full mitigation potential of India’s cities, regulatory, policy, and operational mea- sures need to be developed. For some sectors, this governance and institutional framework still needs to be elaborated. SWM, for example, lacks a fully coherent framework, although various laws, poli- cies, standards, and guidelines exist. Improvements are needed in technical and emission standards for disposal facilities as well as waste minimization and diversion policies. There is a need to stop open dumping and introduce mandatory design specifica- tions and standards for the development, operation, and monitoring of disposal infrastructure. Realizing urban mitigation potential for other sec - tors will require enforcement and implementation of already existing standards and regulations. The housing sector, for instance, has broadly applica- ble policies that include India’s National Mission on Sustainable Habitat

and implementation of already existing standards and regulations. The housing sector, for instance, has broadly applica- ble policies that include India’s National Mission on Sustainable Habitat (NMSH) and NMSH 2.0, which identify green buildings as a key policy intervention area. Several building policies and codes encour - age energy efficiency in residential and commercial buildings. These include the Model Building Bylaws of 2016, the revised National Building Code of 2016, the updated Energy Conservation Building Code of 2017, and the Eco-Niwas Samhita of 2018. Energy-efficient building design bylaws should be integrated into existing municipal codes to improve implementation. State governments have also developed state-level guidelines for buildings. Finally, more data and information should be made available to inform decision making on mitiga- tion strategies and performance. For example, in the housing sector, city-level databases of building typologies, condition, age, and

should be made available to inform decision making on mitiga- tion strategies and performance. For example, in the housing sector, city-level databases of building typologies, condition, age, and energy efficiency could be developed. Data on energy consumption in buildings should be made publicly available. Baseline carbon emissions should be established at the neigh- borhood level through available diagnostic measures to identify priority interventions. Progress on the implementation of energy-efficient building codes and appliances as well as energy audits at the build- ing block level should be monitored. This kind of information will allow policymakers to better gauge the pros and cons of specific interventions across sectors.

83 03 OPPORTUNITIES fOR EffICIENT, RESILIENT AND LOW-CARbON URbAN DEvELOPmENT BOX 3.2 Improving Low-Carbon Development in Indian Cities: A Four-point Agenda Climate resilient and low-carbon development in Indian cities can best be achieved by four key improvements: (i) urban compactness; (ii) improving municipal services, especially solid waste management; (iii) green and energy efficient housing; and (iv) investing in public transport: Urban Compactness Indian cities will need to develop a strategy to achieve compactness. Cities can better manage their expansion. This will require a spatial planning and design paradigm that facilitates increasing density in built-up areas through several mechanisms. First, density should be enabled through sustainable TOD. This involves the promotion of multi- modal mobility through a system of public spaces, bike paths, and public transport routes. Second, density should be facilitated by incentives for vertical development, using zoning and building

modal mobility through a system of public spaces, bike paths, and public transport routes. Second, density should be facilitated by incentives for vertical development, using zoning and building code regulations that encourage mixed- use development and taller buildings. Third, cities should seek out opportunities for redevelopment and adaptive reuse. The streamlining of approval processes can help transform brownfield sites. Fourth, cities should optimize land use, reduce construction and plan appropriate landuse in risk prone areas such as green recreational spaces in areas exposed to flooding that can provide public parks and absorb excess flood water. Improving Municipal Services, Especially SWM India will need to invest in the provision of green and resilient municipal services to curb urban emissions, spe- cifically from SWM. Reducing service sector emissions will require local mitigation roadmaps with clear priorities, milestones, and timeframes to facilitate fundamental

to curb urban emissions, spe- cifically from SWM. Reducing service sector emissions will require local mitigation roadmaps with clear priorities, milestones, and timeframes to facilitate fundamental changes. For instance, for the SWM sector, significant invest- ments are needed to sustain current levels of waste management, acquire separation and disposal infrastructure, and advance low-carbon alternatives. Landfilling, with adequate methane recovery features, is the most financially viable solution. Waste generation needs to be minimized through behaviour change, recycling, and reuse. It will be essential to establish a system with environmental controls that can absorb increasing volumes over time to curb GHG emissions. 84 TOW ARDS RESILIENT AND PROSPEROUS CITIES IN INDIA BOX 3.2 (cont.) The financial sustainability of municipal services needs to be improved to facilitate these changes. Indian cities have inadequate cost recovery for municipal services. Service charges are often not even sufficient to cover opera- tion and maintenance (O&M) costs. A sample of 14 large and medium-sized cities shows that, in 2021, they recovered less than 50 percent of O&M costs for municipal water supply services. Cities like Amritsar recover only one quarter or less. This undermines financial sustainability and the ability of cities to invest in service delivery. Greater empha- sis needs to be placed on creating and enhancing revenue sources. Fiscal transfers to service delivery agencies— including SWM agencies—need to be predictable and based on a clear set of priorities. Green and Energy-Efficient Housing There are significant opportunities for reducing emissions in the housing sector. Among green housing measures,

predictable and based on a clear set of priorities. Green and Energy-Efficient Housing There are significant opportunities for reducing emissions in the housing sector. Among green housing measures, the installation of solar panels on rooftops is the most cost-effective way to achieve both energy efficiency and emissions reduction. Green housing interventions should initially target high-income housing types, which have the highest rates of energy consumption and emissions. The government should also consider applying planning regulations to promote more compact, multifamily housing development to reduce the urban carbon footprint while meeting the growing housing demand through high-density housing development. Although green housing interven- tions have a cost, payback periods are usually much shorter than the lifespan of the housing asset. Since housing generates emissions for its entire lifespan, these interventions need to be taken sooner rather than later. Public Transport

usually much shorter than the lifespan of the housing asset. Since housing generates emissions for its entire lifespan, these interventions need to be taken sooner rather than later. Public Transport Well-planned shifts in urban transport can help Indian cities transition to low carbon pathway. The challenge of transitioning urban passenger transport is considerable. There is a growing need for reduced emissions and for bet- ter air quality, yet urban passenger travel demand is continually increasing, expected to rise by a further 42 percent by 2030. A modal shift toward non-motorized and public transport in cities would help reduce overall GHG emissions significantly while improving air quality. TOD and compact city concepts can reduce emissions by up to 20 percent against Historical Trend scenarios. Multimodal integration can help provide seamless connectivity from first to last mile, making public transport journeys faster, safer, and more affordable, increasing their

Trend scenarios. Multimodal integration can help provide seamless connectivity from first to last mile, making public transport journeys faster, safer, and more affordable, increasing their competitiveness against personal transport. Improvements in road transport, together with improved municipal waste management and a transition to cleaner energy, would greatly help reduce the high levels of air pollution. 86 This chapter assesses finance needs to support climate action in Indian cities and examines how the public and private sectors can help to close the financing gap and scale up urban climate solu- tions. Chapters II and III analyzed the ways Indian cit- ies can adapt to and manage the increasing impacts from urban flooding and warming, as well as ways to incorporate low-carbon development in urban plan - ning and management. This chapter assesses how to finance the needed climate actions up to 2070. The chapter is divided into three sections. Section 4.1 provides an overview of the finance needs of India’s cities for climate action, building on previous analyses that have been updated and expanded for this report. Section 4.2 provides an overview of the public sector role in driving and financing urban cli- mate solutions. Section 4.3 discusses the role of the private sector in implementing and financing these solutions, highlighting the barriers encountered and making

driving and financing urban cli- mate solutions. Section 4.3 discusses the role of the private sector in implementing and financing these solutions, highlighting the barriers encountered and making recommendations for improving the role of private finance. 4.1 URBAN FINANCE NEEDS FOR CLIMATE ACTION IN INDIA In light of India’s rapid urbanization and the window of opportunity to integrate climate action into all future growth, this section examines the investment needs for climate-resilient and low-carbon urban infra- structure and services up to 2050 and 2070. Estimating Investment Needs for Climate-Resilient and Low-Carbon Urban Infrastructure and Services Estimates of financing needs for climate actions in this chapter are based on an analysis conducted for this report at two levels of investment: (1) the investment in urban infrastructure to current stan - dards that will be needed to account for the expected increase in the urban population by 2050 and 2070; and (2) the investment

(1) the investment in urban infrastructure to current stan - dards that will be needed to account for the expected increase in the urban population by 2050 and 2070; and (2) the investment needed to make cities climate-resilient. The first step was to assess urban infrastructure investment costs assuming current standards. This step calculated the percentage share of India’s GDP Based on the conservative urbanization projection of reaching 43% urbanization by 2050 and 52% by 2070, as much as $2.4 trillion could be needed by 2050, and $10.9 trillion by 2070. This step built on the 2011 work of GoI’s High Powered Expert Committee (HPEC) which estimated investment requirements for urban infrastructure services nationwide.74 The HPEC study estimated India’s total investment needs for urban infrastructure and municipal services at the equivalent of 1.14 percent of GDP in 2031. This first step also considered the similar World Bank 2022 estimate75 of urban infrastructure investment needs of an average 1.18 percent of GDP for 2021-2036. Taking these benchmarks, future urban infrastruc- ture investment needs were worked out for 2050 and 2070 as a percentage share of GDP. These estimates assumed better-quality urban infrastruc - ture and an increased share of the total population projected to be living in urban areas. It was assumed that, by 2050, the quality of urban infrastructure of Class IB municipal

urban infrastruc - ture and an increased share of the total population projected to be living in urban areas. It was assumed that, by 2050, the quality of urban infrastructure of Class IB municipal bodies would equal that of Class IA municipal bodies, 76 and that the quality of urban infrastructure in smaller municipal bodies would be improved to a minimum of the standard for Class 1B municipal bodies by the year 2070. Two urbanization scenarios were assumed: (i) a conservative urbaniza- tion projection (the urban population would reach 43 percent of India’s total population by 2050 and 52); and (ii) a moderate urbanization projection (the urban population reaching 54 percent of the total popula- tion by 2050 and 66 percent by 2070). In the second step, the additional costs for making urban infrastructure low-emission and climate-re - silient were identified. In recent years, the national government has supported many urban “mis- sions,” including those for “smart cities,” and states have developed state adaptation plans. This update to India’s existing NDC is a step forward towards its long-term goal of reaching net-zero by 2070. India has developed multiple climate mitigation and disaster risk management policies. GoI has announced over 150 climate policies at the national level since 2005, many of which are comparable to advanced instruments in high-income countries. 82 Climate mitigation policies cover a range of sectors and diverse policy instruments. In the 1990s and 2000s, the focus was on agriculture, but from 2010 forward, the policy focus extended to energy, transport, and con - struction. Initially, the main instruments were strategic planning, the establishment of an institutional frame -

but from 2010 forward, the policy focus extended to energy, transport, and con - struction. Initially, the main instruments were strategic planning, the establishment of an institutional frame - work, and setting product and industry standards, but in more recent years, a much broader range of policy instruments has been deployed to reduce emissions and support low-carbon technologies. The results of these policies have been consid- erable. India has drastically reduced deaths from extreme events. Improvements in weather forecast - ing and the development of early warning and evac - uation systems have successfully reduced human losses from climate-related events, with up to 94 percent reduction in cyclone-related deaths in the last 20 years. 83 In addition, India’s power sector has achieved a paradigm shift toward renewables, driven by concerted policy efforts and declining costs. Development of new renewable energy capacity has exceeded new coal power development every year since

Disaster risk management needs to be better aligned with urban planning and development. Disaster risk management policy and practice have developed relatively recently and separately from the urban planning and development paradigm of Indian cities. The National Disaster Management Act was adopted in 2005 (GoI, 2005), and State and District Disaster Management Plans were approved in 2007 and 2014, respectively. The National Disaster Management Plan (NDMP) was adopted in 2019. These instruments have led to the formation of national, state, district and local level institutional structures. The Prime Ministerial Council on Climate Change, created in 2007, mandated the preparation of the NAPCC,91 which was released on June 30, 2008. Its purpose is to enable India to adapt to climate change and enhance the ecological sustainability of the development path. The NAPCC proposes eight national “missions,” which are outlined below. These missions are multi-pronged, long-term, and integrated strategies for achieving key goals in the context of climate change. They provide the overall framework and support for climate action at state and city level. Centrally Sponsored Plans for Urban Development GoI has sponsored multiple “urban missions” in partnership with state and local governments. These centrally sponsored plans include the: (i) Smart City Mission (SCM); (ii) two phases of the Atal Mission for Rejuvenation and Urban Transformation (AMRUT); (iii) two phases of the Swachh Bharat Mission (SBM); and (iv) Jawahar Lal Nehru Urban Renewal Mission (JNNURM). State Action Plans for Climate Change In 2009, following the launch of the NAPCC, the central government directed states to draft SAPCCs that were aligned with the NAPCC. The objective of SAPCCs is to document the unique vulnerabilities to climate risks at state level and to lay out planned interventions that both respond to physical climate change and activate a transition to a lower-carbon economy. These interventions may include strategies for mitigation and adaptation across different sectors, such as

both respond to physical climate change and activate a transition to a lower-carbon economy. These interventions may include strategies for mitigation and adaptation across different sectors, such as agriculture, transport, and energy. As of July 2023, all 28 states had published at least one ver - sion of their SAPCCs, covering 98 percent of the Indian population. The level of ambition and comprehensiveness of the strategies differs across states. Some states have released thorough action plans with state-level vulnerability assessments, timelines, and budget estimates; others rely on national data for their vulnerability assessments and outline only high-level solutions.99 The Climate Center for Cities GoI has set up a one-stop-shop—the Climate Centre for Cities (C-Cube)—to help integrate climate considerations into urban planning and infrastructure investment. With the aim of mainstreaming climate actions in Indian cities and establishing a one-stop shop for climate-informed urban

climate considerations into urban planning and infrastructure investment. With the aim of mainstreaming climate actions in Indian cities and establishing a one-stop shop for climate-informed urban development, the National Institute of Urban Affairs (NIUA) with support from the Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs (MoHUA) established the C-Cube in June 2020. The vision of the Centre is to “build climate actions in cities.” There are two objectives: (i) to help cities mainstream climate actions through collaboration with stakeholders and improved planning and impact monitoring; and (ii) to integrate climate variability and climate change considerations in all urban infrastructure projects. C-Cube has helped create partnerships and improve readiness. C-Cube has developed the Climate Smart Cities Assessment Framework and has surveyed 126 large cities in India, with more surveys in progress. It has created partnerships with national and international organizations and practitioners who

Cities Assessment Framework and has surveyed 126 large cities in India, with more surveys in progress. It has created partnerships with national and international organizations and practitioners who are providing technical assistance (e.g., city Climate Action Plan [CAPs], disaster management plans, flood and water stagnation risk assessments, water resource management plans, vulnerability assessments, GHG inventories, etc.). The survey of 126 cities 100 shows a national average “readiness” score of just 30 percent. Clearly, Indian cities need to do much more to improve climate actions (see Box 4.2). To support urban development in the coming years under con - servative growth projections, even without taking any account of climate issues, the analysis in Section 4.1 above (see Table 4.1) shows that India would need to more than double the share of the GDP it allocates for urban infrastructure development, up from the cur - rent 0.68 percent to 1.52 percent by 2050 and to 1.95 percent by 2070. If urbanization trends are higher, a higher share of GDP would need to be allocated: 1.89 percent by 2050 and 2.46 percent by 2070. When climate-related investment is factored in, even higher shares of GDP will need to be allocated. Estimates are that, to achieve climate-resilient and low-carbon infrastructure investment, between 1.67 and 1.95 percent of GDP would be required by 2050, and between 2.13 and 2.48 percent would be required by 2070. achieve climate-resilient and low-carbon infrastructure investment, between 1.67 and 1.95 percent of GDP would be required by 2050, and between 2.13 and 2.48 percent would be required by 2070. Again, needs would be significantly higher if urban areas grow faster (see Table 4.1). Limited Public Finance and Low Absorptive Capacity Most urban infrastructure is financed through intergovernmental fiscal transfers, but only a small part has been climate-related. Total capital investment in cities has averaged only $10.6 billion per year in the past decade (2011–18). Compare that to the approximately $175–204 billion per year that will be needed by 2050 (see Table 4.1). State trans- fers and own source revenues (OSR) are low and com- mercial financing plays a minor role. Most financing for urban infrastructure comes from GoI fiscal sup - port, largely from the urban missions discussed (Box 4.1). A small share of this support is for climate-re- lated investment. In addition to the urban

urban infrastructure comes from GoI fiscal sup - port, largely from the urban missions discussed (Box 4.1). A small share of this support is for climate-re- lated investment. In addition to the urban missions, finance for urban climate-related investment has also been available from the Faster Adoption and Manufacturing of Hybrid and Electric Vehicles (FAME) scheme and from a Finance Commission scheme for water and sanitation and air quality improvement. 103 Kelly, White, and Anand, 2020. Absorptive and implementation capacity for urban infrastructure delivery is low. Large cities across India have generally not been able to fully spend their capital budgets in recent years. A review of 10 large urban local governments across the country showed that the cities were able to spend only two-thirds of their cumulative capital budget over the previous three fiscal years (World Bank, 2022). Slow execution on several recent flagship GoI urban missions points to constraints in implemen -

two-thirds of their cumulative capital budget over the previous three fiscal years (World Bank, 2022). Slow execution on several recent flagship GoI urban missions points to constraints in implemen - tation capacity. For example, over the last six fiscal years, cities across India have executed only about one-fifth of the cumulative cost of approved proj- ects under the SCM and AMRUT. The overall scale of financing at city levels is rel- atively low, with OSRs contributing a declining share and cities increasingly dependent on fiscal transfers. The total income of all ULBs nationwide from all sources remained flat at around 1 percent of GDP during the FY2011 to 2018 period. OSRs contrib - uted a declining share, dropping from three-quarters of total municipal revenue nationwide to two-thirds over the same period. The cause was the slow growth in OSRs relative to the fiscal transfers from GoI and state governments on which cities have become increasingly dependent. Property taxes and

over the same period. The cause was the slow growth in OSRs relative to the fiscal transfers from GoI and state governments on which cities have become increasingly dependent. Property taxes and charges for municipal services contribute well below global norms. The growth in property tax collection (the largest OSR) has been particularly slow. Property tax represents a far lower share of GDP (0.15 percent) than is the average for low- and middle-income countries (0.3–0.6 percent of GDP).103 In addition, cost recovery by urban utilities is also on average lower than in comparator coun - tries such as Brazil and Mexico. Low service charges for municipal services undermine the financial sus- tainability and viability of infrastructure investments. The low levels of OSRs constrain all urban infra- structure investment and services and would act as an impediment to raising the scale of urban financing required for climate action. Revisions in

96 TOW ARDS RESILIENT AND PROSPEROUS CITIES IN INDIA BOX 4.3 Urban Local Government Finance Sources for Climate Actions Cities can get finance for climate action from various sources, the choice of which should be determined by project needs and local gov - ernments’ capacity. These include private and public institutions that may be domestic or international. International public sources of finance for climate action include global cli- mate funds, multilateral development banks, and bilateral financing. They need sovereign guarantees from national governments to get access to funds. In contrast, domestic sources, including national development banks and municipal revenue, offer more direct access to funds through long-term concessional loans and grants. They still, however, require that local governments have robust systems, accountability frameworks, and creditwor - thiness to attract private investors. Domestic public sources of climate finance include national development banks

local governments have robust systems, accountability frameworks, and creditwor - thiness to attract private investors. Domestic public sources of climate finance include national development banks and national and regional climate funds. Private sources of finance for climate action include debt markets for bonds, commercial banks for loans, private and impact investors for equity finance, and philanthropic foundations and non-governmental organizations (NGOs) for grant funding. Apart from grants, all forms of private finance are provided in exchange for financial returns. Sources may either be domestic or interna- tional, depending on the entity, and they require a higher return on their financing than providers of public financing. Private finance is, therefore, more expensive to take on than public finance. Source: Local Government Climate Finance Instrument (World Bank, 2024).

Many Indian cities are making progress in climate actions (see Box 4.2). To accurately track spending and attract further financing for climate action, accurate budgeting and monitoring will be vital. As seen from the review of budget and accounting statements of selected cities discussed earlier, it is difficult to assess how much Indian cities are currently spending on activi - ties that meet climate goals due to a lack of classifi - cation and tracking of these activities. Clearly, Indian cities should improve and harmonize the tracking of climate spending in a consistent manner, “tagging” these expenditures. Guidance from the central and state governments could help with this tracking and, in turn, help attract

the tracking of climate spending in a consistent manner, “tagging” these expenditures. Cities should explore climate-specific global sources of finance and technical assistance that are currently largely untapped. Cities can explore international and domestic public and private financing sources (see Box 4.3). Global sources of finance for climate action include various climate funds. For certain climate activities, it may be pos- sible for cities to tap into voluntary carbon markets. They could thus receive carbon credits for emis- sions reductions from governments and private sec- tor organizations aiming to offset their emissions.

97 04 fINANCINg CLImATE RESILIENT URbAN DEvELOPmENT IN INDIA Organizations like C40, the Global Covenant of Mayors for Climate and Energy (GCOM) and the City Climate Finance Gap Fund specialize in helping cities identify and finance their climate investment needs through technical assistance. “Tagging” of municipal expen - ditures as climate-related, as discussed above, could help cities attract these sources of finance and tech- nical assistance. Central and state governments have an important role to play in connecting cities with these global institutions to facilitate their access to these global resources. Improving Public Sector Role in Urban Climate Action and Financing The national and state governments can help in meeting significant investment needs. The first step is to develop a national roadmap, working with the states and key counterparts. The roadmap could not only identify dedicated programs for managing flood and urban heat, but also actions to ensure a low-carbon

a national roadmap, working with the states and key counterparts. The roadmap could not only identify dedicated programs for managing flood and urban heat, but also actions to ensure a low-carbon pathway and to identify financing needs and prepare a financing strategy. One approach might be to develop a new “urban resil- ience mission.” The roadmap can focus on identify - ing different sources of financing and funding for climate action that Indian cities can target such as improving budgets, market-based financing, carbon market revenues, and private investments, as well as Land Value Capture (LVC), tax incremental financing (TIFs), redirection of fossil fuel subsidies, commer - cial revenue, and other innovative methods. It can identify what is needed to build local capital markets in achieving significant resilient urban infrastructure goals. 99 04 fINANCINg CLImATE RESILIENT URbAN DEvELOPmENT IN INDIA 4.3 THE ROLE OF THE PRIVATE SECTOR IN URBAN CLIMATE SOLUTIONS AND THEIR FINANCING Private Finance of Urban Infrastructure Private sector financing is slowly but gradu- ally increasing with the deployment of innova- tive business models and financing instruments that provide risk mitigation. At present, private sources finance only 5 percent of the infrastructure investment in Indian cities—3 percent as PPPs and 2 percent as commercial debt. 107 However, private investment is gradually gaining traction, particularly for solutions that have: (i) reduced upfront costs and assured revenue streams via innovative business models such as a lease or a fee-for-service model; (ii) financing mechanisms to mitigate risk such as guar- antees, blended finance, subordinated debt, line of credit, etc. and (iii) proven demand aggregation with policies in place, such as extended producer respon- sibility (EPR).108 Private sources of financing

blended finance, subordinated debt, line of credit, etc. and (iii) proven demand aggregation with policies in place, such as extended producer respon- sibility (EPR).108 Private sources of financing for climate action for urban areas could be budgets, market-based financing, carbon market revenues and private investments including: (i) debt finance through com- mercial banks and capital markets; (ii) equity finance through private developers or private equity firms; (iii) infrastructure funds; (iv) carbon markets; and (v) green bonds and impact funds. Apart from financing and funding, the private sector also has other critical roles in providing climate solutions—as a technology provider of low-carbon solutions (e.g., e-mobility, SWM), management efficiency provider in construc - tion and operations of assets, better quality provider of urban services, financier, and insurance provider. In the five major cities assessed, energy consumption and emissions from the housing sec - tor are anticipated to increase 2.4 times by 2050 and 4.1 times by 2070 if no mitigation interventions are introduced. Green housing investments such as rooftop solar panels and building envelope interven- tions can greatly reduce energy consumption and emissions, but at a significant cost. However, sim - ulation results show that the capital costs can be recouped within a relatively short period. the same five cities shows that the Best Case scenario mitigation and adaptation package would require additional capital investment costs. Interventions to address urban heat, such as urban greening, heat early warning systems, and cool roof programs, are key entry points. They all have costs, but all also have high cost–benefit ratios. Certain types of urban infrastructure and ser - vices are more suitable for private sector invest- ment in Indian cities, while others are better suited to public investment or to PPP arrange - ments. 104 TOW ARDS RESILIENT AND PROSPEROUS CITIES IN INDIA This chapter presents an action plan for Indian cit- ies and key stakeholders to achieve climate-resil- ient and low-carbon urban development. It builds on the findings of earlier chapters that identified the needs in Indian cities to: (i) adapt to the rising impacts of urban flooding and extreme heat together with the related remedial actions; (ii) incorporate low-carbon development in urban planning and management; and (iii) mobilize the public and private sectors to scale up urban climate solutions and financing. This chapter first sets out the background, highlight- ing the need to strengthen urban resilience actions in India. Then in sets out key recommendations for city interventions in Section 5.2; national and state level interventions in Section 5.3; for develop a national financing strategy in Section 5.4; and improving stakeholder collaboration in Section 5.5. The Section 5.6 outlines critical next steps. 5.1

level interventions in Section 5.3; for develop a national financing strategy in Section 5.4; and improving stakeholder collaboration in Section 5.5. The Section 5.6 outlines critical next steps. 5.1 STRENGTHENING URBAN RESILIENCE IN INDIA Indian cities need to seize the opportunity to leverage the urbanization process to improve cli- mate resilience and achieve low-carbon, inclusive growth. With the rapid development of cities and urban infrastructure in the coming years, there is an opportunity to maximize development gains by ensuring that all decisions and investments reflect climate risks and lead to resilient urban development. This chapter identifies key actions that could ensure that cities adapt to climate effects and reduce car - bon emissions while delivering sustainable high eco- nomic returns and improved well-being for citizens. Investing in urban resilience and low-carbon growth will not only help with inclusive development but also better quality of urban life and

eco- nomic returns and improved well-being for citizens. Investing in urban resilience and low-carbon growth will not only help with inclusive development but also better quality of urban life and cleaner air. Timely actions can avert billions of annual damages in flood- ing and urban heat-related impacts. The simulations conducted for this report also show that the most ambitious urban policies and related investment would allow Indian cities to reduce their emissions by an average of 80 percent by 2050 and, through compact urban development, preserve a total of 337 square kilometers of land from urban development in five cities studied. A reduction of up to 70 percent of GHG emissions can be achieved in the SWM sector alone by 2050. A dedicated program is needed to support resilient urban development in India. Key priorities include investing in a 10-point action plan for cities, devel- oping a national and state urban resilience program to address flooding and extreme heat, and

urban development in India. Key priorities include investing in a 10-point action plan for cities, devel- oping a national and state urban resilience program to address flooding and extreme heat, and devis- ing a financing and implementation strategy. The investment program need to improve institutional capacity and incentives for improving collaboration and cooperation of key stakeholders. Implementing this program would not only ensure that cities adapt to climate effects and reduce carbon emissions but also deliver sustainable high economic returns and improved well-being for citizens. 5.2 INVEST IN A 10-POINT CITY CLIMATE ACTION PLAN Cities are on the front line of climate impacts and can play a key role in GHG mitigation. This report recommends 10 key actions that each Indian city can take to plan for and invest in climate solutions (see Box 5.1). The scope of these actions can be adjusted based on the local context, population and size, climate and disaster risk profile,

city can take to plan for and invest in climate solutions (see Box 5.1). The scope of these actions can be adjusted based on the local context, population and size, climate and disaster risk profile, development trajectory, and urban local government capacity. See details in Annex 3.

105 05 AChIEvINg CLImATE RESILIENT URbAN DEvELOPmENT IN INDIA 1 Conduct and continue updating local climate and disaster risk assessment. All climate action needs to be grounded in an evi- dence-based assessment of risks and likely cli- mate impacts. Climate adaptation, mitigation, and risk management measures need to be based on up-to-date risk assessments, dynamic risk profiles, and GHG assessments. Based on these assessments and risk profiles, cities can prepare strategies for urban resilience, city-level adaptation, and low-car - bon development. Many Indian cities are already improving risk information and factoring risk assess- ments into strategies for resilient urban develop - ment. Box 5.2 illustrates the example of Chennai. Urban development strategies need to mainstream integrated approaches to strengthening resilience with a particular focus on the poor and vulnerable and to prioritize low-carbon development. Within these strategies and plans, risk information should be

approaches to strengthening resilience with a particular focus on the poor and vulnerable and to prioritize low-carbon development. Within these strategies and plans, risk information should be used to identify robust and integrated adapta- tion and mitigation measures covering both green and grey infrastructure using integrated rather than standalone approaches. The risk assessment and the planning process need to be coordinated with sectoral and master planning measures to ensure coordinated and efficient urban development. (See the example of Chennai in Box 5.2 and that of Buenos Aires, Argentina in Box 5.3.) In both cases, strategies for adapta- tion and mitigation actions are based on continu- ous assessments of climate risk and GHG emissions. The CAP was based on a

Low-carbon Growth The Tamil Nadu government launched Chennai’s first CAP114 in June 2023, mak - ing Chennai only the second Indian city to have a Paris Agreement-aligned CAP. The CAP was based on a thorough risk assessment which identified the key cli- mate risks as floods, rising sea levels, and heat. The CAP mapped out which parts of the city are most susceptible to each particular risk and set out a strategy for the necessary adaptation and pre- vention measures. Figure 5.1, the flood hazard and vulnerability map, shows an example of this mapping. The CAP also pinpointed vulnerable communities and areas, acknowledging that the effects of climate change will particularly affect poorer areas of Chennai. The climate action planning process also led to the development of a GHG emis- sions inventory and the identification of strategies and actions for low-carbon growth and mitigation. The CAP provides for continuous data gathering on emis- sions and traces out a roadmap for the city to

inventory and the identification of strategies and actions for low-carbon growth and mitigation. The CAP provides for continuous data gathering on emis- sions and traces out a roadmap for the city to become carbon-neutral by 2050. The CAP sets targets of 1 percent reduc - tion by 2030 compared to 2018-19, 40 percent reduction by 2040, and achieving net zero by 2050. The Chennai Metropolitan Development Authority (CMDA) is responsible for urban planning, which is a cross-cutting disci- pline affecting land use decisions, green spaces, transport, housing, and more. The CMDA is thus the main institution for delivering increased resilience and emissions reductions. The CMDA aims to embed key actions from the Chennai CAP in the Third Chennai Development Master Plan (2027-2046) and to do this in alignment with the national and state governments’ ambitions on climate action and the UN Sustainable Development Goals. 114 Chennai Climate Change Action Plan. 108 TOW ARDS RESILIENT AND PROSPEROUS CITIES IN INDIA 2 Develop impact-based, multi- hazard and inclusive warning and response systems With the recent experience of climate impacts and the sharply rising disaster damages and losses, it is essential to strengthen urban early warning and response systems. The focus needs to be on improv- ing impact-based and inclusive multi-hazard warning and emergency response systems, strengthening last-mile connectivity and community preparedness, developing recovery systems, and ensuring safety nets for poor and low-income households. Cities can build on the existing systems, including on recent improvements in national flood forecasting and warning, to develop inclusive and impact-based warning and response systems. India has invested and benefited from improved disaster warning and response systems at national and regional levels; for example, the Orissa cyclone response system has saved many lives. However, more is needed to strengthen urban

from improved disaster warning and response systems at national and regional levels; for example, the Orissa cyclone response system has saved many lives. However, more is needed to strengthen urban warning and response services. BOX 5.4 Flood Forecasting and Early Warning System in Kolkata, India In order to mitigate floods, Kolkata Municipal Corporation (KMC) worked to establish a city level Flood Forecasting and Early Warning System (FFEWS). The FFEWS is designed to collect and show

India In order to mitigate floods, Kolkata Municipal Corporation (KMC) worked to establish a city level Flood Forecasting and Early Warning System (FFEWS). The FFEWS is designed to collect and show real-time information on temperature, air quality, and water stagnation and other climate-related data using 400 sensor nodes. These ultrasonic sen - sors were installed across vulnerable hotspots and near critical junctions, including but not limited to canals, water pumping stations, traffic junctions, and schools. Communities were also involved in the process. Residents agreed to install shopfront sensors in commercial areas. The sensors capture real-time information on various environmental parameters and upload the data onto a centralized cloud server for processing. The data collected can be visualized on a Geographic Information Systems (GIS) platform. When warnings are triggered, they are made available to the public, especially in risk-prone areas and for vulnerable community

can be visualized on a Geographic Information Systems (GIS) platform. When warnings are triggered, they are made available to the public, especially in risk-prone areas and for vulnerable community groups, through mobile notifications and radio and television broadcasts. FFEWS also has the potential to develop various flood scenarios with the collected data to inform future flood risk. The comprehensive FFEWS is an effective intervention to monitor and disseminate flood risk data. Through crowdsourc- ing, this data can be made accessible to large groups of people through smartphones and other digital interfaces. Sources: Catalogue of Best Practices for Building Flood Resilience. GFDRR (January, 2022). Available at: https://www.gfdrr.org/en/publication/cata- logue-best-practices-building-flood-resilience; and “Kolkata Gets India’s First Comprehensive City-Level Flood Forecasting and Warning System.” ADB (September 2018). 110 TOW ARDS RESILIENT AND PROSPEROUS CITIES IN INDIA 3 Ensure climate-sensitive new urban development With the urban population set to double by 2070, it is essential that new urban development integrate cli- mate risk and low-carbon development potential from the outset. Cities need to ensure that the supply of new urban land is based on local climate and disas - ter risk profiles, and to take measures to ensure risk-sensitive development with access to resilient services, infrastructure, and buildings. Flood risk reduction in new build areas requires good zoning for flood areas and other hazards and must include provi - sions to reduce exposure to flood hazard and to improve storm water management. It must also pro - vide risk identification for surrounding areas within the catchment or basin. At the housing level, cities must develop an integrated and evidence-based policy and regulatory framework for improving climate adap- tation in the housing sector and to mainstream adap -

or basin. At the housing level, cities must develop an integrated and evidence-based policy and regulatory framework for improving climate adap- tation in the housing sector and to mainstream adap - tation planning and resilient investment planning at the habitat level. At the same time, climate-sensitive urban development needs to take account of the grow- ing hazards to urban road networks, where even lim - ited network exposure to flooding can result in drastic urban mobility disruptions. It is essential to strengthen inter-agency coordination and risk-based planning, considering hazards when planning new infrastructure investments, and accounting for both the criticality and the exposure of the asset. Box 5.5 illustrates the broad-based strategy for handling climate risk and transitioning to a low-carbon pathway through the urban planning process in Yokohama, Japan. BOX 5.5 Integrating Climate Risk and Transitioning to a Low Carbon Pathway through Innovative Urban Planning in

to a low-carbon pathway through the urban planning process in Yokohama, Japan. BOX 5.5 Integrating Climate Risk and Transitioning to a Low Carbon Pathway through Innovative Urban Planning in Yokohama, Japan The combination of Yokohama’s topography (consisting of hills, plateaus and lowlands surrounded by rivers and coasts) and urbanization patterns (where hills, forests, and agricultural land have been developed into residen - tial and commercial land) has exacerbated Yokohama’s exposure to flood risk. Learning the lessons of 2004, when Yokohama Station suffered severe damage from torrential rain from Typhoon Ma-on, the city implemented adap - tation measures. These included storm sewer trunk lines to allow the Station and its surrounding areas to sustain severe flooding that would be caused by up to 74 mm/hour rainfall (approximately the rainfall of a 30-year return period). In 2017, the area was designated as the first “urban flood hazard zone” in the country. Regarding mitigation

by up to 74 mm/hour rainfall (approximately the rainfall of a 30-year return period). In 2017, the area was designated as the first “urban flood hazard zone” in the country. Regarding mitigation efforts, Yokohama aims to achieve large-scale CO 2 reduction and to reach carbon neutrality by 2050 through the combi- nation of municipal efforts, actions by busi- nesses and research organizations in the city, and citizen action. Ongoing efforts include: (i) promotion and implementation of zero emis- sion buildings/housing (ZEB/ZEH); (ii) transport modal shifts to public transport; (iii) adoption of electrical vehicles/fuel cell vehicles (EVs/ FCVs); and (iv) selection of low-carbon electric - ity and area energy management through artifi- cial intelligence (AI) and Internet of Things (IoT) and pilot testing of Environmental Model Zones, which balance highly convenient urban func - tions with carbon reduction. Disaster Management Authority; TCPO: Town and Country Planning Organization; UDD: Urban Development Department; and ULB: Urban Local Body. 4 Invest in risk reduction measures, for floods and heat Climate change and urbanization patterns are increasing disaster risk in Indian cities, and the complexity of the causal factors requires a focus on integrated rather than standalone approaches. A range of solutions would typically be required, with a shift from hard engineering to hybrid solutions spanning both grey and green infrastructure. Responses to the growing hazard of floods need to be risk-based. Given the huge needs, national and state governments can primarily focus on “hot spots”—areas where large climate impacts and future urbanization are expected. Cities can give priority

112 TOW ARDS RESILIENT AND PROSPEROUS CITIES IN INDIA to new areas for development. What is required are flood management policies and action plans based on risk profiles that assess flood risk not only at city level but at the basin or watershed level (or at the level of coastal zones) and that avoid development patterns that increase flood risks. Integrated urban flood man- agement projects with NbS can be a very effective response. See the example from Beira, Mozambique, in Box 5.6). Indian cities also face water scarcity challenges and need comprehensive solutions that improve urban watershed management, and water storage capacity. See the example from Cape Town in Box 5.7, and Shizouka city, Japan, in Box 5.8). 115 https://www.nrdc.org/sites/default/files/ahmedabad-heat-action-plan-2018.pdf A range of investments can help adaptation to the growing problem of extreme heat in cities. Climate projections indicate a substantial increase in episodes of extreme urban heat stress that

A range of investments can help adaptation to the growing problem of extreme heat in cities. Climate projections indicate a substantial increase in episodes of extreme urban heat stress that will lead to rising heat-related deaths and economic losses without any remedial action. The introduction of HAPs in cities such as Ahmedabad has saved lives and reduced loss of economic output. See Box 5.9. BOX 5.6 Urban Flood Resilience in Beira, Mozambique In Beira, Mozambique, the Cities and Climate Change Project financed conventional grey interventions, such as upgrades and extensions of inadequate drainage systems, together with NbS, which focused on restoring the capacity of the Chiveve River to mitigate floods in Beira. At the heart of the NbS approach was a park designed to reduce flooding for over 50,000 inhabitants while hosting recreation areas, a botanical garden,

the Chiveve River to mitigate floods in Beira. At the heart of the NbS approach was a park designed to reduce flooding for over 50,000 inhabitants while hosting recreation areas, a botanical garden, playgrounds, and public facilities. Overall, the project provided flood protection benefits for 234,000 people. Soon after completion of the project, the city was hit by Cyclone Idai, one of the strongest cyclones ever to hit the African continent. The project’s investments proved resilient, demonstrating the effectiveness of combining green and grey infrastructure. Note: Parts of Beira, Mozambique before and after the Cities and Climate Change Project. Source: Building Resilience Through Green-Grey Infrastructure: Lessons from Beira. World Bank, January 2022. 114 TOW ARDS RESILIENT AND PROSPEROUS CITIES IN INDIA BOX 5.7 Managing Water Scarcity through Improved Storage in City of Cape Town, South Africa Cape Town experienced a 1-in-a-500-year drought from 2015 to 2018, which laid bare the city’s limited capacity to access alternative sources of water. The city adopted coping strategies to deal with the short-term water scarcity issue, using demand management measures to reduce water demand and reallocating water in storage. The city then developed a new water strategy for the longer term. This will add other sources of storage and water to its portfolio to relieve overdependence on current systems. The strategy includes both built infrastructure in the form of desalination plants and wastewater reuse facilities as well as green storage through the increased use of groundwater and groundwater storage. These measures will be combined with investments to increase the resil- ience of the regional water storage system to create an integrated,

the increased use of groundwater and groundwater storage. These measures will be combined with investments to increase the resil- ience of the regional water storage system to create an integrated, multiple-water-source approach to mitigate future drought impacts. Source: What Has the Future in Store? A New Paradigm for Water Storage. (World Bank, 2023). 116 TOW ARDS RESILIENT AND PROSPEROUS CITIES IN INDIA BOX 5.9 Ahmedabad Heat Action Plan Close to 1200 deaths have been avoided after adopting HAP in Ahmedabad. It is also being replicated in other cities of India with the leadership of National Disaster Management Agency (NDMA). The HAP has four key area of focus: (i) effective early warning and inter-agency response plan; (ii) public awareness and outreach; (iii) building capacity of medical professionals; and (iv) reducing heat exposure and promoting adaptative measures. Source: “Extreme Heat in Cities: From assessment to action” Session at Understanding Risk Global Forum 2024. 5 Prioritize the most vulnerable including the urban poor It is essential to prioritize support to the poorest and most vulnerable citizens, including through targeting and safety nets. Poor neighborhoods and households suffer the greatest impact of disasters because they have limited adaptive capacity and thus experience much higher losses in terms of

targeting and safety nets. Poor neighborhoods and households suffer the greatest impact of disasters because they have limited adaptive capacity and thus experience much higher losses in terms of well-being. Therefore, urban adaptation measures need to prioritize poorer communities and neigh - borhoods. Most important are early warning and response systems, as well as adaptive social protec- tion systems that can identify and target the most vulnerable groups in advance and safety nets for those affected during and after a climatic shock (see Box 5.10 for an example of inclusive warning and evacuation system in New Orleans, USA). Risk reduction measures include infrastructure upgrad - ing and home improvements. Strong community participation is essential.

118 TOW ARDS RESILIENT AND PROSPEROUS CITIES IN INDIA 6 Facilitate the private sector’s role in risk transfer and resilience Government needs to create an “enabling ecosys - tem” to facilitate much greater private sector engagement and investment. Private sector engage- ment in the financing and investment of urban infra - structure is limited and there are significant constraints of policy and regulatory barriers, low private sector interest, limited cash flow potential, and lack of accessible financial instruments. The gov- ernment can support the development of private sec- tor engagement and financing by creating an “enabling ecosystem” to strengthen the policy and regulatory environment and build a robust framework to catalyze finance and regulate compliance. City governments can also benefit from private sector investment in valuable city land by transforming a flood-prone area into a high-value commercial and residential area (see Box 5.11 for an example from Dar es Salaam,

also benefit from private sector investment in valuable city land by transforming a flood-prone area into a high-value commercial and residential area (see Box 5.11 for an example from Dar es Salaam, Tanzania). BOX 5.11 Maximizing Value from a Flood-Prone Area: The Msimbazi Basin Development Project in Dar es Salaam, Tanzania The project seeks to demonstrate how value can be created by transforming a flood-prone area near the city center into a lively green space and commercial and residential area. The project involves a process in which the govern - ment, with the support of World Bank financing, invests in flood mitigation and stabilizes 400 hectares of regularly flooded prime land in the Lower Msimbazi River Basin, where flooding is expected to worsen in the future because of climate change. The intervention will create more than 300 hectares of a wetland park serving the dual purpose of recreational amenity for the city and stormwater attenuation and filtration facility, with

change. The intervention will create more than 300 hectares of a wetland park serving the dual purpose of recreational amenity for the city and stormwater attenuation and filtration facility, with more than 60 hectares of flood-protected terraces—attractive for a range of commercial and residential uses—available for vertical development. Once the land is stabilized, the government will take charge of planning the urban development in Lower Msimbazi, granting development rights to private investors who are interested in developing the 60-hectare land portfolio on the flood-protected terraces. This process is expected to unlock significant value in the Lower Msimbazi that can be captured through the sale of land and development rights and thus allow for recovery of the initial public investments in flood mitigation. Source: Transforming Tanzania’s Msimbazi River from a Liability into an Opportunity. (World Bank, August 2019). Available at: https://www.world-

119 05 AChIEvINg CLImATE RESILIENT URbAN DEvELOPmENT IN INDIA 7 Plan and invest in compact and green city expansion and densification Cities need to plan for and invest in compact and green city expansion and densification. As urban population growth drives urban expansion, there is an opportunity to improve efficiencies in Indian cities through a new spatial planning and design paradigm. This paradigm provides for a combination of a “compact city” that saves land and reduces costs of infrastructure and service provision, incorporates low-carbon construction standards, builds in renew - able energy solutions, and optimizes the use of public transport, cycling, and walking through TOD. All these features contribute to mitigation and adaptation goals. Box 5.12 describes the example of the “green and compact city of Singapore.” 6.
