120 TOW ARDS RESILIENT AND PROSPEROUS CITIES IN INDIA 8 Plan and invest in efficient, resilient, and green municipal services Successfully pursuing low-carbon development pathways will require investing in the provision of green and resilient municipal services. Reducing service sector emissions requires local mitigation roadmaps with clear priorities, milestones, and time - frames to facilitate fundamental changes. One key ser- vice for low-carbon development is waste management where continuing on the current trajectory would keep emissions at around current levels. But there is high potential for emissions reduction that could signifi - cantly contribute to India’s GHG mitigation targets. What is needed for waste management is to use a wide range of technologies to minimize carbon dioxide and methane emissions along the value chain. Significant investments will be needed to sus- tain current levels of waste management, acquire separation and disposal infrastructure, and advance

and methane emissions along the value chain. Significant investments will be needed to sus- tain current levels of waste management, acquire separation and disposal infrastructure, and advance low-carbon alternatives. Landfilling, with adequate methane recovery features, is the most financially BOX 5.12 The Green and Compact City of Singapore Singapore is an example of a city that has adopted a compact and biodiversity-friendly approach to urban planning. Despite its population density, which increased from 3,538 residents per km2 in 1970 to 7,810 residents per km2 in 2020, the city managed to expand green areas from 36 percent to 47 percent of its total land area. In fact, since the early 1960s, Singapore has had a strong ambition to green itself in order to become a highly livable and competitive city. For example, high-rise greenery has increasingly become an essential component of the city’s development plan, due in part to the limited amount of land available. The government now

city. For example, high-rise greenery has increasingly become an essential component of the city’s development plan, due in part to the limited amount of land available. The government now requires property developers to replace any greenery lost during construction and covers 50 percent of the costs of installing green roofs and walls on existing buildings. As a result, the city’s 72 hectares of rooftop gardens and green walls are set to triple by 2030. These amenities, combined with 4,172 hectares of green space (parks and park connectors), reduce the city’s heat-island effect, help absorb stormwater, provide space for recreation, and increase urban biodiversity. 121 05 AChIEvINg CLImATE RESILIENT URbAN DEvELOPmENT IN INDIA viable solution. It will be essential to establish a sys- tem with environmental controls that can absorb increasing volumes over time. Waste generation also needs to be minimized through behavior change, recy- cling, and reuse. The financial sustainability of munic- ipal services needs to be improved to facilitate these changes, with greater emphasis placed on creating and enhancing revenue sources and with predictable and prioritized fiscal transfers to service delivery agencies, including SWM agencies. Involvement of the private sector can bring efficiency and innovation gains and financing, and projects can be eligible for finance from global sources (see Box 5.13 for an exam- ple from City Of Monterry, Mexico). BOX 5.13 City of Monterrey, Mexico—Municipal Waste to Energy and GHG Emissions Reduction Project The city of Monterrey, Mexico, with the support of the Global Environment Facility (GEF) and the World Bank, devel-

of Monterrey, Mexico—Municipal Waste to Energy and GHG Emissions Reduction Project The city of Monterrey, Mexico, with the support of the Global Environment Facility (GEF) and the World Bank, devel- oped a solution to address the city’s issues with waste management and electricity provision. To implement inte- grated waste management in the participating municipalities, a PPP was established. Private operators of landfills contracted by the municipalities were required to collect recyclable materials and utilize biogas to generate electric- ity at a more competitive price than the national grid. While the landfills and equipment remained the property of the municipalities, operations were contracted to the private sector. The waste-to-energy landfill initiative was eligible for funding from the Clean Development Mechanism of the Kyoto Protocol, and the profitability of bundling such various activities as energy generation, recycling, and public works attracted private sector

from the Clean Development Mechanism of the Kyoto Protocol, and the profitability of bundling such various activities as energy generation, recycling, and public works attracted private sector participation. The initial investment for the project amounted to $25 million, with 53 percent from private funding. The partnership received a grant of $6 million from GEF, which improved the internal rate of return of the project from 13 percent to 28 percent. Results have been positive: Between 2004 and 2020, the initia- tive reduced CO2 emissions by 5.7 MtCO2eq and generated financial savings for the municipality worth several million dollars. This was achieved by providing low-cost electricity to power the metro rail system and public lighting. The revenues generated were then used to support a proof-of-concept solar energy supply to public schools in a low-in- come community within the metropolitan area. Source: World Bank, 2023. Indian cities can benefit from transitioning urban transport and achieving co-benefits of synergies between urban mobility and air quality improvements through: (i) a modal shift toward non-motorized and public transit; (ii) TOD and compact city concepts; and (iii) multimodal integration to provide seamless connec - tivity. 123 05 AChIEvINg CLImATE RESILIENT URbAN DEvELOPmENT IN INDIA 10 Encourage resilient and green buildings and housing There are opportunities to promote and incentiv - ize resilient and green housing and buildings. Since housing generates emissions for its entire lifespan, these interventions need to be taken sooner rather than later, initially targeting high-income housing types which use the most energy. Although green housing investments will require incremental costs upfront, the extra costs of solar and green housing are relatively limited and comprise only a small share of the large total investments in housing in the coming decades. It is critical to support informal settlements and low-income housing in improving resilience and energy efficiency measures since they are most vul- nerable to climate shocks. Box 5.14 describes how an integrated approach to energy efficiency in a Buenos Aires informal settlement reduced energy use, water consumption, and energy use embedded in

nerable to climate shocks. Box 5.14 describes how an integrated approach to energy efficiency in a Buenos Aires informal settlement reduced energy use, water consumption, and energy use embedded in materials. It also shows how policy and practices can also tackle climate mitigation in informal settlement upgrading and retrofitting. BOX 5.14 Energy-efficient, Affordable Housing in Buenos Aires Improve public spaces and energy efficiency measures in Buenos Aires, Argentina. In the Autonomous City of Buenos Aires in Argentina, Villa 31, one of the oldest informal settlements, is home to 35,000 people. The World Bank, through the Metropolitan Buenos Aires Urban Transformation Project, provided technical assistance with support from the Energy Sector Management Assistance Program to pursue energy effi- ciency measures in housing, infrastructure, and service delivery. This included energy-efficient housing retrofits in four neighborhoods for 75,000 residents that provided insulation,

energy effi- ciency measures in housing, infrastructure, and service delivery. This included energy-efficient housing retrofits in four neighborhoods for 75,000 residents that provided insulation, weatherproofing, solar heating systems and LED street lighting. Taken together, these improvements resulted in 20 percent less energy use, 20 percent less water consumption, and 20 percent less embodied energy in materials. Further, the Centro de Desarrollo Emprendedor y Laboral (CeDEL), a job training center that helps residents and local business owners, was Argentina’s first project to achieve EDGE certification, a certification system focused on making residential and commercial buildings in emerging market countries more resource efficient. Source: Project Summary available at: https://projects.worldbank.org/en/projects-operations/project-detail/P159843

Key acronyms: IMD: India Meteorological Department; MoHUA: Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs; MoRTH: Ministry of Road Transport and Highways; NDMA: National Disaster Management Authority; NIUA: National Institute for Urban Affairs; SDMA: State Disaster Management Authority; TCPO: Town and Country Planning Organization; UDD: Urban Development Department; and ULB: Urban Local Body. 5.3 INVEST IN NATIONAL AND STATE URBAN RESILIENCE PROGRAMS ADDRESSING FLOODING AND EXTREME HEAT The national and state governments have the opportunity to launch urban resilience programs that create an enabling environment, support pol - icy reforms, and provide technical, financial, and operational support to high-risk states and cit

opportunity to launch urban resilience programs that create an enabling environment, support pol - icy reforms, and provide technical, financial, and operational support to high-risk states and cit - ies. National flood and heat resilience programs are proposed in this report. This national approach to both flooding and heat is needed because these cli - mate-related and climate-induced risks typically extend beyond urban jurisdictional boundaries and require higher level and cross-sectoral coordination. In the case of urban flood management, while some investments and actions (such as maintenance of the urban drainage system) fall under the purview of the local government, critical aspects such as manage - ment of the watershed and the river basin and coastal management may fall under the state or national line ministry. National and state programs can not only develop solutions for the higher level and trans-jurisdic - tional problems but can also help identify the appropriate

the state or national line ministry. National and state programs can not only develop solutions for the higher level and trans-jurisdic - tional problems but can also help identify the appropriate intervention for cities—for example, cities with low present risk but high future risk should focus on interventions such as NbS and risk-sensi- tive urban planning; cities with both high present and high future risk should embed flood protection into both existing urban areas and areas of expansion; and cities with both low present and low future risk can focus on maintaining drainage infrastructure to keep it functional in the decades to come (see sec - tion 2.3 and Figure 2.11). For urban heat, while many cities such as Ahmedabad have successfully imple- mented HAPs, many small and medium size cities will need the expertise and resources to prepare and implement local heat action planning and measures, and a larger national or state level program is needed. A supporting initiative is

size cities will need the expertise and resources to prepare and implement local heat action planning and measures, and a larger national or state level program is needed. A supporting initiative is needed to improve insti- tutional capacity on urban resilience. Support in improving government staff numbers and their skills in climate resilient and low-carbon development is needed together with the national and state pro- grams on flood and extreme heat. A national urban climate resilience hub can support local government capacity building. In addition, organizational respon- sibilities need to be clarified. Responsibilities for urban resilience are often fragmented, and limited local governments have departments that address hazards like urban flood risk. The planning capacity of urban local governments needs to be enhanced, particularly for spatial planning, risk-sensitive urban

125 05 AChIEvINg CLImATE RESILIENT URbAN DEvELOPmENT IN INDIA land use planning, and infrastructure planning. It will be necessary also to develop capacity in data collection and risk assessment, including hydrome- teorological and geospatial data, to factor this into planning, and to build the systems and capacity for preparedness, response, and recovery in case of extreme events and disaster. A national hub for collecting and monitoring cli- mate resilience and GHG data is needed, together with a national facility to support project prepara- tion and implementation. The CITIIS and NIUA has been playing a key role in this regard and can con - tinue to support Indian cities. State-level initiatives can be supported by existing networks of urban local governments. Department; ULB: Urban Local Body Priority: Immediate Priority: Next 1 year; Short term: 1 to 5 years; Medium to Long term: 5 to 10 years 5.4 DEVELOP A FINANCE STRATEGY FOR CLIMATE RESILIENT URBAN DEVELOPMENT The high cost of investing in climate-resilient and low-carbon cities underscores the need for financial support. Future urban growth will cost a considerably higher share of GDP. To finance the infrastructure that will be required to accommodate the doubling of the urban population by 2070, India will need to rapidly increase the share of GDP allocated to urban infrastructure investments, even without taking into account climate-related needs. When climate-related investment is factored in, even higher shares of GDP will need to be allocated. Achieving climate-resilient and low-carbon infrastructure investment of up to 2 percent of GDP would be required by 2050 and up to 2.5 percent would be needed by 2070. Apart from additional fiscal transfers, private, or international

infrastructure investment of up to 2 percent of GDP would be required by 2050 and up to 2.5 percent would be needed by 2070. Apart from additional fiscal transfers, private, or international finance can also be considered. The total costs will be considerably above current lev - els: India could expect to spend more than $2 trillion between now and 2050 on urban infrastructure that is climate resilient and low-carbon and up to $10 trillion or more, cumulatively, by 2070. If the pace of urban - ization accelerates, the nation might have to spend some $2.5–2.9 trillion by 2050, and around $12 trillion or more by 2070. Most of the financing will have to come from the public purse, while private sector, or international sources can also play a big role. One TABLE 5.1. (cont.)

Specifically, to finance climate-related investment, cities should explore cli - mate-related global sources of finance and technical assistance that are currently largely untapped. To improve the financial sustainability of public services, which also typically lack adequate funding, ULBs need to apply efficiency measures and develop higher levels of cost recovery and own source revenue. In order to accurately track climate spending and to attract further climate financing, accurate bud- geting and monitoring will be vital. Current urban climate-related investment is very limited and is often not captured by the accounting system. Even where cities are taking additional climate action, many climate-related investments are not system - atically tracked, impeding both monitoring and the attraction of financing. Indian cities should improve and harmonize the tracking of climate spending in a consistent manner, “tagging” these expenditures. Guidance from the central and state governments could help with this tracking, and this could in turn help attract climate financing. BOX 5.15 Kenya’s Fiscal Transfers to Support Local Climate Action Kenya’s Financing Locally-Led Climate Action (FLLoCA) Program-For-Results provides dedicated, ringfenced finance for climate action, predominately at the decentralized level. Some 90 percent of program commitments are targeted at the county and community level. The program supports partnerships between local governments and communi- ties to assess climate risks and identify socially inclusive solutions tailored to local needs. This ensures that finance is available to match local needs and local decisions, and that support for climate resilience reaches those most at risk, including women, youth, persons with disabilities, the elderly, and other

is available to match local needs and local decisions, and that support for climate resilience reaches those most at risk, including women, youth, persons with disabilities, the elderly, and other traditionally marginalized groups. The remaining 10 percent of program funds is allocated to building the capacity of national systems to support these activities. FLLoCA required a national policy reform that made it a requirement for counties to make county-level climate change action plans. These were developed through participatory methodologies endorsed by county governments. 132 TOW ARDS RESILIENT AND PROSPEROUS CITIES IN INDIA All levels of government need to prioritize inter - sectoral climate adaptation planning and develop- ment actions. These are relatively new concerns for Indian cities, and they are yet to be fully integrated within urban planning policies and programs. The role of the central government to support national policies and programs on urban planning and devel- opment aligned to macroeconomic parameters at the national level. State governments are responsi- ble for: (i) defining urban development policies at the state level; (ii) establishing local government institu- tions to implement the urban policy agenda; and (iii) supporting urban programs and projects. The basic national framework for urban planning and develop- ment plans and policies, established in the Town and Country Planning Act, empowers ULBs to prepare and implement plans. Cities will need to cooperate beyond their boundaries on certain regional, national, or

and policies, established in the Town and Country Planning Act, empowers ULBs to prepare and implement plans. Cities will need to cooperate beyond their boundaries on certain regional, national, or international issues. Several areas may require cooperation at the state or national level, or even at the international level. These areas include: flood management, where a watershed or river basin or coastal management approach may be required; and SWM, where inter-ju- risdictional approaches may be required to tackle waste and debris flowing in waterbodies across juris- dictions. An issue requiring cooperation across inter- national boundaries is air and water pollution. Much of northern India, for example, is part of the Indo- Gangetic Plain and Himalayan Foothill (IGP-HF) region of South Asia, which requires regional cooperation to address air and water pollution. Cities will also need to develop, through education, a pattern of popular engagement in green, sustainable approaches. Overall, involve- ment of the communities is essential to developing a better understanding of climate impacts, to ensure compliance with regulations such as building bylaws, and to build awareness and engagement in disaster preparedness and response. 133 05 AChIEvINg CLImATE RESILIENT URbAN DEvELOPmENT IN INDIA BOX 5.18 Role of Key Stakeholders in Advancing Climate-Resilient and Low-Carbon Urban Growth Achieving a climate-resilient and low-carbon transition in India’s cities will require the support of all stakeholders. The Role of National and State Governments A national program, a national hub, and a national support facility The national government can launch national programs that create an enabling environment, support policy reforms, and provide technical, financial, and operational support to high-risk states and cities. National flood and heat resilience programs are proposed in this report. This national approach to both flooding and heat is needed because these climate-related and climate-induced risks typically extend beyond urban jurisdictional boundaries and require higher-level and cross-sectoral coordination. A national hub for collecting and monitoring climate resilience and GHG data is needed, together with a

urban jurisdictional boundaries and require higher-level and cross-sectoral coordination. A national hub for collecting and monitoring climate resilience and GHG data is needed, together with a national facility to support project preparation and implementation. The NIUA has been playing a key role and can continue to support different levels of Indian cities. State-level initiatives can be supported by existing networks of urban local governments. Support to policy and operational frameworks and capacity building National and state governments can strengthen integrated regulatory, policy, and operational frameworks and improve state and local capacities to effectively plan for and implement resilient housing, SWM, and urban infrastructure. One critical sector for national and state government support is housing, where India has a first-rate opportunity to develop climate-resilient and green housing on a massive scale. This is because the country is likely to double its housing stock

support is housing, where India has a first-rate opportunity to develop climate-resilient and green housing on a massive scale. This is because the country is likely to double its housing stock by 2070, building more than 144 million new dwelling units. Yet the housing sector is highly vulnerable to climate impacts due to overcrowding, obsolete construction, poor-quality building materials, unplanned settlements in high-risk zones, and inadequate basic services. These problems are closely linked to poor compliance with building bylaws resulting from the lack of coordinated action by local authorities, lack of local technical capacity, and lack of enforcement and penalties for non-compliance. What is needed is for national government to work with states and cities to introduce integrated regulatory, policy and operational frameworks, and to improve state and local capaci- ties to effectively plan for and implement resilient and low-carbon green housing. Similarly, the SWM sector needs

policy and operational frameworks, and to improve state and local capaci- ties to effectively plan for and implement resilient and low-carbon green housing. Similarly, the SWM sector needs national and state government support to develop a fully coherent framework for an efficient and much lower carbon future. What is needed are regulations to stop open dumping and mandatory design specifications and emissions standards for the development, operation, and monitoring of low-carbon dis- posal infrastructure. Financial strategies Transparent, predictable, and accountable strategies for climate-targeted intergovernmental fiscal transfers and performance-based grants would provide cities with the ability to plan and appropriate funds for climate actions. While transfers from public revenue constitute the largest share of urban local government funding, they are often subject to challenges that limit their efficiency and intended outcomes. Transfers may lack predictability, have unclear

constitute the largest share of urban local government funding, they are often subject to challenges that limit their efficiency and intended outcomes. Transfers may lack predictability, have unclear allocation formulas, and may often be delayed. Streamlining and improving the clarity of transfer programs or systems will encourage cities to better plan their budgets—both in the short and the longer term. Furthermore, while many cities are taking additional climate actions, climate investments are often not tracked, impeding mon - itoring of spending on activities that meet climate mitigation and adaptation goals, and making it more difficult to attract financing. Indian states and cities have started to harmonize and improve the tracking of climate budgeting. Strengthening this process will allow cities to track progress and to access climate performance-based grants, incen- tivizing cities to enhance their capacities and performance.

The Role of Urban Local Governments The Role of the Private Sector Urban local governments have primary responsibility for climate planning and action. Institutional strengthening is a vital area of focus. This includes improving local government staff numbers and their skills in climate-smart develop- ment, strengthening financial capacity, climate budgeting and tracking, and improving access to finance for climate action. Urban local governments also have a key role in generating awareness and improving the enabling environ - ment for private sector and civil society involvement. Information and Integration Local governments have a critical role in data collection and in evidence-based policy making at the city level, so climate adaptation measures can be tailored based on risk profiles of specific urban areas. It is essential that cities develop and improve city-level climate data to allow for evidence-based and

so climate adaptation measures can be tailored based on risk profiles of specific urban areas. It is essential that cities develop and improve city-level climate data to allow for evidence-based and risk-informed decision making to tailor climate adaptation measures to the local risk profiles. For example, effective urban flood risk mitigation measures should address the city’s risk exposure based on (i) geographic characteristics (inland/coastal, mountainous/low - land, vegetation coverage, infiltration capacity of soils); (ii) urbanization characteristics (population density, built-up area, land-use patterns, sprawl patterns); (iii) flood risk characteristics (fluvial, pluvial, or coastal); and (iv) existing hazard management measures (presence and maintenance of drainage, regulations, early-warning and emergency preparedness). Similarly, cities need to adopt measures to tackle urban extreme heat using an evidence-based under- standing of extreme heat event trends and on a mapping

early-warning and emergency preparedness). Similarly, cities need to adopt measures to tackle urban extreme heat using an evidence-based under- standing of extreme heat event trends and on a mapping of hot spots of UHI effects. Investment and Implementation Realizing the mitigation potential in cities requires policies and measures to be prioritized based on the city’s car - bon emission inventory, urban growth form, and development path. Successfully pursuing low-carbon development pathways will require investment in urban infrastructure and systems that significantly contribute to India’s GHG mit- igation targets. The majority of carbon emissions in urban areas are from the energy, transport, and building sectors. These emissions vary depending on multiple factors including urban density and the structure of a city’s economy. It is crucial to understand the city’s carbon emission inventories, baselines, and development scenarios to devise realis- tic mitigation targets and measures.

and the structure of a city’s economy. It is crucial to understand the city’s carbon emission inventories, baselines, and development scenarios to devise realis- tic mitigation targets and measures. The striking possible results cited in the introduction to this chapter —reduction of emissions by 80 percent by 2050 and significant reduction in development on greenfield sites—require a strategic blend of measures if they are to be achieved, including a significantly cleaner electricity mix, reduced energy demand, greater urban density, electrification of transport, efficient waste management strategies, efficient urban amenities, and cooling strategies such as green spaces. Innovation The private sector has a key role in enabling innovation; supporting the provision of green, efficient, and resilient municipal services and buildings; and providing innovative financial instruments and insurance for flooding and other hazards. Involving the private sector and developing strategies to

and resilient municipal services and buildings; and providing innovative financial instruments and insurance for flooding and other hazards. Involving the private sector and developing strategies to improve cities’ access to finance will help the transition toward the low-carbon growth path. Access to financing sources and instruments differs depending on the city’s needs, its fiscal autonomy and capacity, the incentive framework, and its creditworthiness.

The new urban resilience program can focus on incentivizing and supporting cities for improving flood and urban heat resilience while ensuring a low-carbon development pathway. Leadership and dedicated effort will be required to develop the program together with funding and an implementa- tion strategy based on the overall climate financing needs. The program will require public and private sector engagement and a robust results framework to monitor progress. A large-scale engagement with communities, including improved, communications will also be crucial to improving public support for planned actions. Recommendations in this report can support ongo- ing initiatives by the GoI. There are critical syner - gies between the recommendations from this report and the ongoing efforts under the recently launched City Investments to Innovate, Integrate, and Sustain (CITIIS) 2.0 Program,

are critical syner - gies between the recommendations from this report and the ongoing efforts under the recently launched City Investments to Innovate, Integrate, and Sustain (CITIIS) 2.0 Program, which supports (i) strengthening of urban climate resilience in selected cities (includ- ing on waste management and circular economy, flooding, cyclones, etc.), (ii) development of state- level CAPs and climate budgets through state level urban climate cells; (iii) bespoke capacity building support, including the Leadership in Climate Change Management Program (LCCM); and (iv) development of practical and easily digestible toolkits, templates, and workbooks. CITIIS may benefit from incorporat - ing the recommendations provided in this report. The key findings of the report need to be translated into practical guidance for cities. As a next step, it is important that key recommendations from the report are translated into practical guidance for Indian cit - ies. This would be similar to the

into practical guidance for cities. As a next step, it is important that key recommendations from the report are translated into practical guidance for Indian cit - ies. This would be similar to the “Streets for People— Pathways of Change from India’s Smart Cities” (also known as the Smart Streets Workbook, https:// itdp.in/resource/streets-for-people-pathways-of- change-from-indias-smart-cities/), which provides case studies of transformational mobility interven - tions in more than 50 streets across 40 cities, includ- ing actionable ideas and insights on financing models to scale up interventions. Targeted communications for select themes such as urban flooding, urban heat, climate governance, and adaptation, among others, may be useful to cities. A high-level example of what might be included in the toolkit or workbook is shown in Figure 5.1.

137 05 AChIEvINg CLImATE RESILIENT URbAN DEvELOPmENT IN INDIA 5.7 CONCLUSION Indian cities have a significant opportunity to adapt to growing climate and disaster impacts and set the country on a path towards vibrant eco- nomic growth and prosperity. With the urban pop - ulation expected to double by 2050, increasing from 480 million to 951 million, more than half of India’s urban growth is still to come. Actions are needed now to better prepare cities in guiding a climate-re- silient, low-carbon manner to avoid the high cost of future disaster damages and losses, impacts, and the expense of retrofitting infrastructure. There must be an immediate focus not only on preparing cities for climate impacts but also on shaping new urban growth in a climate-resilient, low-carbon manner. Flooding and extreme heat events are projected to increase significantly in Indian cities. Other cli- mate impacts such as water scarcity and drought, together with other shocks such as earthquakes, are likely

extreme heat events are projected to increase significantly in Indian cities. Other cli- mate impacts such as water scarcity and drought, together with other shocks such as earthquakes, are likely to aggravate projected effects and expose even more people and assets. In many cases, cities are not affected by a single type of climate risk but by com - pounding flood, heat, and drought risks together with ongoing urban stress related to rapid urbanization. 138 TOW ARDS RESILIENT AND PROSPEROUS CITIES IN INDIA Most of the impacts are likely to be borne by the most vulnerable people living in informal settlements or new migrants to the cities. This report delves into the most significant climate risks for Indian cities and identifies the urgent and most cost-effective actions that cities can take to improve their resilience to climate and disaster risks. Chapter 2 provided an overview of key climate risks, tools, and approaches to improve urban resil - ience. Chapter 3 explored key actions to enhance liv - ability in cities while following a low-carbon pathway through efficient and modern urban services, green housing, more green spaces and public amenities, and cleaner air. Chapter 4 provided an overview of financing needs and actions to improve public and private financing and funding for climate actions. This Chapter highlighted best practices from Indian cities, along with global examples, to guide the adoption of relevant urban

improve public and private financing and funding for climate actions. This Chapter highlighted best practices from Indian cities, along with global examples, to guide the adoption of relevant urban adaptation measures. The impacts of climate change are likely to be even more severe than the projections, but with increased awareness and support, Indian cities can continue to drive economic growth while improving the overall quality of life. A transformative and investment program is pro- posed in this report, which can support effec - tive urban policies, institutional frameworks, and capacity improvement on one hand, and raise resources, including from the private sector, on the other. This Chapter summarizes actions and next steps that national, state, and local governments can take and areas where they can work with the private sector and citizens (see Table 5.1 and Annex 3 and 4). The key to lasting change will be having the national and state governments work on the framework for

areas where they can work with the private sector and citizens (see Table 5.1 and Annex 3 and 4). The key to lasting change will be having the national and state governments work on the framework for guidance, support, and finance, and supporting the cities—the local leaders and officers—who can lead urban resilience actions. Making this step change will require systematic support to cities in terms of incentives and capacity building. Tackling the many dimensions of urban challenges and climate change impacts will also require these leaders and officers to make decisions and trade-offs based on the best available information. For this reason, investing in improving digital systems and will also be crucial. Citizen involvement is another key area, and it will be essential for citizens to be involved.

144 TOW ARDS RESILIENT AND PROSPEROUS CITIES IN INDIA Findings from Stakeholder Consultations A roundtable discussion in December 2022 brought together representatives from the GoI, city authori - ties, think tanks, academia, and the private sector as well as the World Bank to deliberate over the ongoing work and identified priorities for resilient and low car- bon urban transition in India. The discussion was held at the World Bank office with participants joining both in person and virtually. The participants reflected on the reform priorities, analytical progress and gaps, constraints, and opportunities in designing and implementing urban resilience programs, including for ensuring inclusion of marginalized urban commu- nities. Key observations emerging from this discus - sion were as follows: On Complexity and Scale of the Challenge » Different city types face different challenges in India. The coastal cities are facing land subsid- ence and infrastructure damage due to flooding

follows: On Complexity and Scale of the Challenge » Different city types face different challenges in India. The coastal cities are facing land subsid- ence and infrastructure damage due to flooding and other hazards; other cities are facing water scarcity and air pollution; hill cities have different challenges. » While the mitigation discussion often centers on carbon dioxide, other emissions such as methane, carbon monoxide, plastic pollution, heavy metal/ toxic compounds pollution, and particulate matter are equally pressing and critical issues for cities. » The fragmentation of functions across multiple departments and levels (national, state, city) is an underlying urban governance challenge in India that also impacts governance of climate risk. This makes the challenge complex, including data col- lection across departments, across sectors (e.g., energy, transport), and for the private sector, which has to deal with multiple stakeholders and regulators. On Urban Planning and

data col- lection across departments, across sectors (e.g., energy, transport), and for the private sector, which has to deal with multiple stakeholders and regulators. On Urban Planning and Climate Adaptation » The urban planning process is currently unable to fully account for environmentally sensitive plan - ning that accounts for water and flood risk man - agement and other resilience priorities. » The insufficient number of urban planners in ULBs and the absence of master plans in several Indian cities undermine effective urban planning and cli- mate risk management. » Urban sprawl is a growing concern for several cit - ies, moving away from a compact form and toward more carbon intensive ways. Traffic management is a need in most cities. » Urban waterfront and river management need attention in Indian cities. On Transport » This is the second largest emitter after industry. A fragmentation of responsibilities affects transport sector actions due to a lack of full ownership by

attention in Indian cities. On Transport » This is the second largest emitter after industry. A fragmentation of responsibilities affects transport sector actions due to a lack of full ownership by city stakeholders. » Non-motorized transport, though promoted by national schemes like AMRUT and SMART Cities, does not receive adequate attention or financing. » Areas where support is needed include: (i) support- ing ULBs in developing good proposals where good PPP models already exist; (ii) bus service plans for Tier 2 cities on a PPP model; and (iii) extending support for operational expenditure (not just cap - ital expenditure). » For e-mobility: • There are many good examples where electric vehicles are not only greener but also profitable (e.g., for delivery companies where e-vehicles can significantly reduce costs per delivery). ANNEX 1. KEY CLIMATE CHANGE CHALLENGES IN INDIAN CITIES

145 ANNEXES » There is a need for trip-data to support better deci- sion making. Data exists with different stakehold- ers and companies for their specific projects. » While personal cars and 2-wheelers are subsidized, private buses are not. There is a case for support - ing businesses that provide electric buses. • City authorities need to add buses based on population projections and at a larger scale than they currently are. On Waste Management » There needs to be a narrative in which waste is seen as a “resource” rather than merely something that needs to be ‘disposed of’. » Recently the private sector has started working in this area but there is a huge need and opportunity to involve more private sector actors. » There is a multiplicity of stakeholders from the generation of waste to final disposal. The World Bank can support this sector by addressing the fragmentation problem through standardized guidelines and frameworks that do not currently exist. » There is also a need to

to final disposal. The World Bank can support this sector by addressing the fragmentation problem through standardized guidelines and frameworks that do not currently exist. » There is also a need to work with future “waste generators.” This is being addressed by some stakeholders by working with children and youth. On Green Buildings » Despite much effort, green buildings only capture 5 percent of the market. The biggest barrier to this is lack of information and awareness at the consumer level, including misinformation that these buildings are costlier. There is a need to work on public and developer perceptions that green buildings can have good payback. » The lack of a “single window” approval process is a big deterrent for adoption of green buildings as the permissions process is very cumbersome. Streamlining this process along with creating a single platform where interested consumers can access the latest technologies and designs is needed. » There is also a need to work on

very cumbersome. Streamlining this process along with creating a single platform where interested consumers can access the latest technologies and designs is needed. » There is also a need to work on strengthening the Energy Conservation Act further. On Data and Analytics » 220 Indian cities are reporting on the Climate Smart City Assessment Framework (CSCAF) data sets on approximately 400 indicators. » Analytics need to ultimately be able to help a city identify what are their specific adaptation options, how to choose from these options, and how it needs to be done. » There is a need for analytics that provide contex - tually relevant and specific solutions because the same climate change challenges (e.g. urban flood- ing) pan out very differently across different Indian cities. » There is existing urban flood modelling work undertaken for cities like Delhi and Kolkata, includ- ing development of a flood early warning system for Kolkata. » A study on climate change impact on micro,

is existing urban flood modelling work undertaken for cities like Delhi and Kolkata, includ- ing development of a flood early warning system for Kolkata. » A study on climate change impact on micro, small, and medium enterprises in India has been car - ried out covering 15 sectors, which highlights the interconnectedness of the urban and rural sectors. While the industries may be in cities, the value chains extend to rural areas. Programming for urban transition must also account for this linkage and integration. On Citizen Engagement » There are no platforms for citizens to engage with and offer solutions to the urban planning and city governance processes with respect to climate change. Some cities have created this as a part of a SMART City scheme where this was mandated, but nothing like this exists for risk governance and climate actions. » There is a need for localized planning, such as ward-level planning, as the impacts of climate change are often localized even within a city.

this exists for risk governance and climate actions. » There is a need for localized planning, such as ward-level planning, as the impacts of climate change are often localized even within a city. » Analytics for the IPCC Assessment Working Group 2 have shown that the greatest gain in well-being in the urban areas can be achieved only by priori- tizing the investment to reduce climate risk for the low-income and marginalized, especially people living in the informal settlements.

146 TOW ARDS RESILIENT AND PROSPEROUS CITIES IN INDIA On Creating an Enabling Environment for the Private Sector » Need to create revenue models that also account for the environmental service and not just financial viability, which would help businesses that have to deal with the complexity of multiple revenue streams (e.g. in waste, different components have different uses) and competition from conventional production lines (e.g. electricity from waste vs. electricity from solar). » PPPs offer great opportunities for addressing com- plex urban challenges. Merging the efficiencies and innovation of the private sector with the resources of the government. These resources could be permitted lands, waste (in the case of the waste industry), or financial support in terms of credit guarantees or capital subsidies. » The financing pool for these projects is immense. Even if we only look at the capital costs, they can - not be met by the private sector alone without the support of

or capital subsidies. » The financing pool for these projects is immense. Even if we only look at the capital costs, they can - not be met by the private sector alone without the support of institutions and lenders. And each of these stakeholders have their own requirements for assurance of financing. » Sectors for urban transition, such as waste man - agement or transport, need “patient capital.” The World Bank Group can support this by facilitating stable structures and partnerships, creating con - vergences between central and state priorities such that limited public monies can be leveraged. Urban Governance Challenges » Fragmentation of functions in the city emerges as the biggest challenge, needing innovative and sus- tained efforts. » Smaller cities do not budget for technical staff for risk assessment or data collection. Even where there are budgets, staff attrition and turnover are very high. » There is no dedicated platform that can bring together city authorities and

staff for risk assessment or data collection. Even where there are budgets, staff attrition and turnover are very high. » There is no dedicated platform that can bring together city authorities and experts on discussions of resilience and low carbon interventions. The people who can be brought together keep changing depending on consultants/experts engaged with the city at that time. Good Practices and Initiatives » Surat has taken the lead in many aspects, such as: • Recycling and reuse of wastewater: 30 percent of tertiary wastewater is being sold to industries such as textiles and power generation, also act - ing as a source of revenue for the municipal cor- poration; intent to scale this up to 70 percent of wastewater by 2030. • Energy efficiency: The only corporation to invest money in renewables which meet 20 percent of the corporation’s energy needs; also investing money in windmill and solar plants; SMC gener - ates 250mw of solar energy through different sources. •

EACH CITY NEED TO INVEST IN CLIMATE RESILIENT, EFFICIENT AND GREEN URBAN DEVELOPMENT 7. Plan for and invest in compact and green city expansion and densification Detailed Recommendations Relevant Agencies Priority Indian cities will need to develop a strategy to achieve compactness and resilient growth. Cities need to better manage their expansion. This will require a spatial planning and design paradigm that facilitates increasing density in built-up areas through several mechanisms: » Density should be enabled through transit-oriented development. This involves the promotion of multimodal mobility through a system of public spaces, bike paths, and public transport routes. » Density should be facilitated by incentives for vertical development, using zoning and building code regulations that encourage mixed-use development and

bike paths, and public transport routes. » Density should be facilitated by incentives for vertical development, using zoning and building code regulations that encourage mixed-use development and taller buildings that follow resilient building code in safe areas. » Cities could seek out opportunities for redevelopment and adaptive reuse. The streamlining of approval processes can help transform brownfield sites. » Cities could optimize land use and forbid construction in risk-prone areas in order to contain reduce exposure to hazards and emissions-intensive sprawl. » Improve provision of urban amenities: new health care, education and public amenities to maximise safety and accessibility. » Improve green public lighting: Promoting the use of LED and other energy efficiency technology for street lighting. ULBs, State and MoHUA Medium to long term or as a part of master plan
